entities.name: Partnership for Global Inclusivity on AI (PGIAI)
entities.notes: The Partnership for Global Inclusivity on AI (PGIAI) is a public-private partnership launched on September 23, 2024, by U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken on the margins of the 79th UN General Assembly in New York City. It brings together the U.S. Department of State and eight leading AI companies — Amazon Web Services, Anthropic, Google, IBM, Meta, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and OpenAI — with a collective commitment of more than $100 million in resources, tools, and funding. The partnership focuses on three pillars: Compute (access to AI models and compute credits), Capacity (building human technical capacity), and Context (expanding local datasets), all aimed at closing the AI access gap in developing countries. The U.S. Department of State committed $10 million in Foreign Assistance and planned an additional $23 million in funding (subject to Congressional approval) to promote responsible AI use and governance globally. Individual company contributions include AWS's $10 million in cloud credits, Google's $120 million Global AI Opportunity Fund, NVIDIA's ~$10 million in free Deep Learning Institute training, and Microsoft's $12 billion in AI infrastructure across Southeast Asia, Africa, and Latin America. The PGIAI is housed under the State Department's Office of the Special Envoy for Critical & Emerging Technology (S/TECH) and complements U.S.-led multilateral AI initiatives including the UN General Assembly's first-ever AI resolution (March 2024) and the Council of Europe's Framework Convention on AI. Three supporting documents — the Risk Management Profile for AI and Human Rights, AI in Global Development Playbook, and Global AI Research Agenda — were developed alongside the partnership to guide the development community.
entities.notes_sources: web.archive.org; preview.state.gov; www.state.gov; 2021-2025.state.gov; blogs.nvidia.com; 2021-2025.state.gov; rivercountrynews.com; www.govconexec.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The PGIAI explicitly emphasizes 'safety, security, and trustworthiness' as core commitments alongside AI deployment, and the State Department has supported international governance frameworks including the Council of Europe's Framework Convention on AI and the UN resolution on AI. The partnership promotes responsible AI use and governance globally, reflecting a balanced moderate stance that pursues both AI access expansion and principled safeguards rather than pure acceleration or restrictive control.

McGovern Foundation (PJMF) is a major philanthropic organization dedicated to advancing artificial intelligence and data science for public benefit, equity, and sustainability. As of 2025, the Foundation has deployed over $500 million in grants over the past decade, ranking it among the largest global supporters of public-purpose AI. In December 2025, PJMF announced a $75.8 million commitment across 149 grants to organizations working in 13 countries on AI for public purpose, climate resilience, human rights, media, journalism, health equity, and digital literacy. Key grantees in the AI policy ecosystem include AI4ALL ($300,000 in 2025 for youth AI governance storytelling), Black in AI ($300,000 for general operating/capacity building), The Future Society ($170,000), Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET), Stanford Digital Economy Lab, ACLU Foundation ($500,000 for AI policy analysis), Center for Democracy & Technology ($500,000), Council on Foreign Relations ($1,000,000 for AI-era governance foresight), and Common Sense Media ($500,000 for youth AI safety). PJMF's grantmaking explicitly targets civic infrastructure, inclusive AI development, AI literacy, and governance frameworks, positioning it as a major force shaping the U.S. and global AI policy ecosystem. Vilas Dhar serves as President of the Foundation.

entities.name: Public First
entities.notes: Public First is a super PAC (FEC ID C00930503) that is part of the broader Public First network founded by former Democratic Congressman Brad Carson and former Republican Congressman Chris Stewart, formed in 2025 to advocate for responsible AI regulation. As of March 2026, the super PAC has raised approximately $1.5 million, with top donors including Michael Cohen ($500,000) and Public First Action ($1 million). The PAC has disbursed funds to aligned PACs including Jobs and Democracy PAC ($740,000) and Defending Our Values PAC ($300,000). It operates as part of a network of three PACs—Public First (bipartisan), Jobs and Democracy (Democratic candidates), and Defending Our Values (Republican candidates)—all supporting candidates who favor AI safety regulation and export controls. The broader Public First Action 501(c)(4) received a $20 million donation from Anthropic in February 2026, with overall network fundraising goals of $50–$75 million for the 2026 midterms. The network directly opposes the pro-deregulation 'Leading the Future' super PAC funded by Andreessen Horowitz, Greg Brockman, and Joe Lonsdale.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Public First explicitly supports targeted AI regulation including export controls on advanced AI chips, transparency requirements for frontier AI labs, child online safety (KOSA), and mitigating AI-enabled bioweapons and cyberattacks. It opposes federal preemption of state AI laws without adequate federal safeguards, and supports minimum safety standards to prevent a race to the bottom among developers. Co-founder Brad Carson stated: 'We have $50 million and 85% of the public sentiment' in support of responsible AI governance.

The fund also co-invested $105 million in Turkish AI platform Insider (May 2023) and led a $196.5 million round for cybersecurity AI company Snyk (December 2022). QIA operates a $1 billion 'Fund of Funds' venture capital program, having already committed nearly $500 million to six VC firms, with eight additional firms under evaluation as of February 2025. QIA maintains a global presence with advisory offices in the United States and Asia Pacific, reflecting its strategy to deploy capital across private equity, public equity, real estate, infrastructure, venture capital, and alternative investments.

entities.name: Runway AI
entities.notes: Runway AI is a New York-based generative AI company founded in 2018, focused on advancing creative tools for filmmakers, artists, and storytellers through artificial intelligence. The company is best known for its AI-powered video generation models, including Gen-1 (2023) and Gen-2, which were among the first of their kind and gained adoption in the TV and film industry, including use by 'The Late Show' on CBS. Runway was also a co-creator of Stable Diffusion, the widely used open-source image generation model. The company has raised approximately $860 million to date, including a $141M Series C extension with participation from Google, NVIDIA, and Salesforce Ventures, and a $315M Series E led by General Atlantic in February 2026, reaching a $5.3 billion valuation. Investors include NVIDIA, Google, Qatar Investment Authority, Felicis Ventures, Lux Capital, Adobe Ventures, and Fidelity Investments, among others. In March 2026, Runway launched a $10M venture fund (Runway Fund) to back early-stage startups in AI, media, and world simulation, having quietly backed companies like Cartesia, LanceDB, and Tamarind Bio. Runway is actively developing 'world models'—next-generation AI systems capable of understanding and simulating physical reality—with applications targeted at medicine, robotics, and autonomous vehicles.

entities.name: Safeguarded AI Programme
entities.notes: The Safeguarded AI Programme is a £59 million (~$60M USD) R&D initiative housed within the UK's Advanced Research and Invention Agency (ARIA), launched in 2024 and led by Programme Director David 'davidad' Dalrymple. The programme sits within ARIA's 'Mathematics for Safe AI' opportunity space and aims to develop quantitative safety guarantees for AI systems—analogous to rigorous safety standards in nuclear power and passenger aviation. Its core thesis involves constructing a 'gatekeeper' AI that uses scientific world models and mathematical proofs to verify that other AI agents operate only within agreed-upon safety guardrails in a given application (e.g., balancing electricity grids, optimising clinical trials). The programme is divided into three Technical Areas: TA1 (Scaffolding, including theory, backend, HCI, and sociotechnical integration), TA2 (Machine Learning, awarded £18M to a single non-profit entity to develop verifiable ML capabilities), and TA3 (Applications, initially funded at £5.4M across ~10 teams to prototype domain-specific deployments). Notably, ARIA redirected its efforts in late 2024 away from TA2 as originally planned, doubling down on TA1 and pivoting TA3 focus toward cybersecurity. The programme has issued multiple solicitations including a £14.2M call for TA1.2 and TA1.3 software developers (deadline April 2025) and a £1M Phase 1 for TA2 organizations. The programme represents one of the most substantial government-funded efforts globally focused specifically on formal verification and guaranteed-safe AI methods.
entities.notes_sources: www.aria.org.uk; www.aria.org.uk; www.aria.org.uk; www.aria.org.uk; www.aria.org.uk; www.find-tender.service.gov.uk; www.linkedin.com; www.c2d3.cam.ac.uk
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The programme does not advocate for restrictive AI regulation per se, but aims to develop the technical safety standards and quantitative guarantees that would underpin responsible AI governance—analogous to engineering safety standards in aviation and nuclear power. This implies support for a structured, standards-based regulatory framework rather than either laissez-faire or prescriptive mandates.

entities.name: Salesforce Ventures
entities.notes: Salesforce Ventures is the corporate venture capital arm of Salesforce (CRM), investing in enterprise software and AI companies. In March 2023, it launched a dedicated $250 million Generative AI Fund focused on funding foundational and enterprise AI companies. Cohere was among the first investments from this new fund, with Salesforce Ventures participating in Cohere's $270 million Series C led by Inovia Capital in June 2023, alongside NVIDIA, Oracle, and others. Salesforce Ventures doubled down on Cohere in a subsequent $450 million round in mid-2024, alongside Nvidia, Cisco, and PSP Investments, at a reported $5 billion valuation. The firm's investment thesis emphasizes enterprise-focused AI companies whose go-to-market approach aligns with Salesforce's own CRM and enterprise software ecosystem. By deploying dedicated generative AI capital and publishing detailed investment rationales on its website, Salesforce Ventures actively shapes the enterprise AI funding landscape and signals strategic priorities for the broader Salesforce platform.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Salesforce Ventures' investment posture—rapidly deploying a $250M generative AI fund and backing foundational model companies like Cohere—reflects a preference for accelerating AI development in the enterprise with minimal friction. No explicit advocacy for heavy regulation is evidenced in the sources; the focus is on commercialization and scaling enterprise AI.
entities.belief_threat_models: Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Power concentration

entities.name: Samsung Electronics
entities.location: Suwon, South Korea (U.S. operations: Taylor, TX and Austin, TX)
entities.notes: Samsung Electronics is a South Korean multinational conglomerate and the world's largest semiconductor manufacturer, uniquely positioned as a leader in both advanced memory and advanced logic chip technologies. In April 2024, the Biden-Harris Administration announced a preliminary memorandum of terms to provide Samsung up to $6.4 billion in direct funding under the CHIPS and Science Act, making it the third-largest CHIPS Act award recipient. Samsung has been manufacturing semiconductors in Texas since 1996, investing $18 billion in its Austin campus over nearly 30 years. In 2021, Samsung announced a minimum $17 billion expansion into Taylor, Texas, and with the CHIPS Act support, total expected investment in the region exceeds $40–45 billion through the end of the decade—one of the largest foreign direct investments for a greenfield project in U.S. history. The CHIPS Act funding supports two chip production facilities, a research center, and an advanced packaging facility in Taylor, Texas, with production expected to begin in 2026, initially at 4-nanometer and eventually scaling to 2-nanometer process nodes. These chips are explicitly cited as essential for AI applications, aerospace, defense, and automotive industries, directly shaping U.S. AI infrastructure capacity. The investment is projected to create over 20,000 jobs and leverage up to $40 million in CHIPS workforce training funding.
entities.notes_sources: commerce.gov; www.reuters.com; apnews.com; news.samsung.com; cnbc.com; www.govinfo.gov; telecompetitor.com; www.cnn.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Samsung has engaged constructively with U.S. government CHIPS Act policy, accepting $6.4 billion in federal funding tied to regulatory and national security requirements. Co-CEO Kyung Kye Hyun emphasized Samsung's role in securing the U.S. semiconductor supply chain for AI chips, suggesting alignment with government-directed industrial policy. However, no explicit public statements on AI regulation specifically have been identified in the available sources.

entities.name: Schmidt Sciences
entities.notes: Schmidt Sciences is a philanthropic organization founded in 2024 by Eric and Wendy Schmidt, bringing together their science-focused philanthropic efforts previously housed under Schmidt Futures. The organization is led by Dr. Stuart Feldman as President and Chief Scientist and prioritizes research across six centers: AI and Advanced Computing, Astrophysics, Biosciences, Climate, Science Systems, and Space. Its flagship AI program, AI2050, is conceived and co-chaired by Eric Schmidt and James Manyika, and supports exceptional researchers working on beneficial AI with the central question: 'It's 2050. AI has turned out to be hugely beneficial to society. What happened?' In December 2024, Schmidt Sciences announced its third cohort of 25 AI2050 Fellows — 5 senior fellows and 20 early career fellows — awarding up to $12 million to researchers across global institutions including MIT, Cornell, Stanford, Oxford, and the University of Toronto. The 2024 senior fellows include David Autor (MIT), Yejin Choi (Stanford), Carla Gomes (Cornell), Roger Grosse (University of Toronto), and Michael Wooldridge (Oxford). Schmidt Sciences also runs the AI Safety Science program (which funded Zico Kolter) and the Science of Trustworthy AI program, reflecting a strong emphasis on safe and beneficial AI development. The organization represents one of the most significant private philanthropic forces shaping academic AI research in the United States and globally.
entities.notes_sources: www.schmidtsciences.org; ai2050.schmidtsciences.org; ai2050.schmidtsciences.org; www.schmidtsciences.org; computing.mit.edu; www.schmidtsciences.org; www.schmidtsciences.org; ai2050.schmidtfutures.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Schmidt Sciences does not explicitly advocate for or against AI regulation, but its funding priorities — emphasizing AI safety, trustworthy AI, and societal benefit — suggest a moderate, responsible-development stance. The AI2050 program explicitly centers safety and effectiveness, and the Science of Trustworthy AI program funds research into making AI systems reliable and accountable. This reflects a belief that targeted, research-grounded approaches to AI governance are appropriate.
entities.belief_threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Labor displacement, Existential risk, Loss of control, Economic inequality

entities.name: Sierra
entities.notes: Sierra is an AI startup founded in 2023 by Bret Taylor (former Salesforce co-CEO and OpenAI board chair) and Clay Bavor (former Google executive who led products like Gmail, Google Drive, and Google Lens). The company builds conversational AI agents for enterprise customer service, enabling brands to deploy AI that handles natural language interactions, real-time issue resolution, and serves as a brand ambassador. Clients include SiriusXM, Sonos, WeightWatchers, and AG1. Sierra reached a $1 billion valuation in January 2024, then raised $175 million in October 2024 led by Greenoaks Capital with ICONIQ and Thrive Capital participating, hitting a $4.5 billion valuation. By September 2025, Sierra raised a further $350 million led by Greenoaks at a $10 billion valuation, bringing total funding to $635 million. Sierra shapes the AI ecosystem by commercializing large language model-based agents for enterprise deployment, directly competing with Salesforce and Microsoft in the AI customer service space.
entities.notes_sources: www.reuters.com; cnbc.com; techcrunch.com; venturecapital.com; www.nbcnewyork.com; www.texau.com; americanbazaaronline.com; stg-aws01pub.cnbc.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance has been stated by Sierra as an organization in the available sources. Co-founder Bret Taylor's public comments focus on the commercial and brand-value potential of AI agents rather than regulatory frameworks, suggesting a commercially oriented, likely light-touch perspective, but this is insufficiently evidenced to assign a firm stance.

entities.name: Simons Foundation
entities.notes: The Simons Foundation is a major private philanthropic organization founded by mathematician and investor Jim Simons and Marilyn Simons, focused on advancing research in mathematics, physical sciences, life sciences, and neuroscience. It funds basic and theoretical science through programs including the Simons Investigators awards, which provide $100,000/year for five years to outstanding theoretical scientists in mathematics, physics, astrophysics, and computer science. The Foundation shapes the AI ecosystem directly through substantial grants: it committed $25 million to fund CUNY and the Flatiron Institute's participation in New York's Empire AI initiative, and supports researchers like Scott Aaronson and William Bialek whose work intersects with AI and computational science. The Foundation operates the Flatiron Institute, an in-house research center with a Center for Computational Mathematics and a Center for Computational Neuroscience, both directly relevant to AI and machine learning research. It also administers the Scientific Software Research Faculty Award, which supports academic scientific software infrastructure — a key AI and computational science resource. Additional programs include the Fellows-to-Faculty Award (formerly Independence Awards), Simons Fellows in Mathematics, and Math+X Investigatorships, all supporting theoretical scientists whose work underpins AI research. The Foundation's total assets and grant-making are on the scale of hundreds of millions of dollars annually, making it one of the most significant private funders of foundational AI-adjacent science in the United States.
entities.notes_sources: www.simonsfoundation.org; www.simonsfoundation.org; www.simonsfoundation.org; www.simonsfoundation.org; www.simonsfoundation.org; www.simonsfoundation.org; www.simonsfoundation.org; www.simonsfoundation.org
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Simons Foundation has not publicly articulated a formal stance on AI regulation. Its participation in New York's Empire AI initiative (a $25M commitment) suggests alignment with state-level public-private AI infrastructure efforts, but no explicit regulatory position is evident from available sources.

entities.name: Tencent Holdings Limited
entities.notes: Tencent Holdings Limited (SEHK: 00700) is one of China's largest and most valuable technology conglomerates, headquartered in Shenzhen, with major businesses in instant messaging (QQ, WeChat), online gaming, social media, fintech, and cloud services. The company is a significant global investor in technology firms, as evidenced by its $300 million investment in Russia's Digital Sky Technologies (DST) in April 2010, acquiring a 10.26% economic interest. Tencent has also backed AI-related companies including Element AI (the Montreal-based AI services firm) alongside Intel and Microsoft, and led a $300 million Series D investment in Reddit in February 2019. In the AI space, Tencent reportedly offered to acquire up to a 20% stake in DeepSeek, the Chinese AI lab, though DeepSeek declined. Tencent has its own substantial AI research and development operations, including Tencent AI Lab, and integrates AI across its product ecosystem. As a major Chinese tech platform and global investor, Tencent shapes the AI ecosystem through both its own AI development and its strategic investments in AI companies worldwide. Its dual role as a Chinese state-adjacent tech giant and global capital deployer makes it a significant actor in debates around AI governance, national security, and technology competition between the US and China.
entities.notes_sources: www.reuters.com; www.prnewswire.com; techcrunch.com; english.cctv.com; www.prnewswire.com; www.themoscowtimes.com; blog.quintura.com; companies.caixin.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Tencent operates under Chinese government regulatory frameworks and has historically aligned with state directives domestically, while pursuing a relatively open global investment strategy. Its regulatory stance on AI is not explicitly articulated in the available sources, but its behavior suggests accommodation of state regulation domestically and market-oriented expansion internationally.

entities.name: The Alan Turing Institute
entities.notes: The Alan Turing Institute is the UK's national institute for data science and artificial intelligence, named after computing pioneer Alan Turing. It serves as a central hub for AI research, convening academic and industry partners across the UK. The Institute plays a key role in administering and co-delivering the Turing AI Fellowships, a £46–54 million initiative launched in 2018 as part of the AI Sector Deal to attract and retain world-leading AI researchers; it directly recruited and administered the first five Turing AI Fellows in 2019. It partners with UK Research and Innovation (UKRI), the Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council (EPSRC), and the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology to deliver fellowship programs, including Turing AI Acceleration Fellowships and Turing AI World-Leading Researcher Fellowships. The Institute hosts Turing Fellows and Turing AI Fellows from universities across the UK, including Cambridge, Leeds, and others, and its university network represents a broad coalition of AI research excellence. Neil Lawrence (DeepMind Professor at Cambridge) holds a Senior AI Fellowship from the Institute, funding his work on machine learning and systems research. As of recent announcements, the Turing AI Fellowship cohort includes 27 researchers spanning diverse AI disciplines including trustworthy AI, biomedical imaging, and drug design.
entities.notes_sources: www.c2d3.cam.ac.uk; inverseprobability.com; www.turing.ac.uk; www.turing.ac.uk; eps.leeds.ac.uk; www.turing.ac.uk; www.ukri.org; www.turing.ac.uk
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Alan Turing Institute's research agenda emphasizes 'trustworthy' and 'secure' AI solutions and works in partnership with government bodies to shape responsible AI development. Its fellowship programs explicitly aim to ensure AI has a 'positive and transformative effect,' suggesting a balanced approach that supports innovation while embedding safety and trustworthiness considerations, consistent with a moderate, targeted regulatory orientation.
entities.belief_threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Privacy, National security, Power concentration

entities.name: The Alignment Project
entities.notes: The Alignment Project is a global AI alignment research funding programme launched in July 2025 by the UK AI Security Institute (AISI), initially with over £15 million in funding. It is backed by an international cross-sector coalition of government, industry, and philanthropic funders including the UK AI Security Institute, Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI), CIFAR, Australian AI Safety Institute, OpenAI, Microsoft, Amazon Web Services, Schmidt Sciences, Anthropic, AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund, Halcyon Futures, Safe AI Fund, Sympatico Ventures, Renaissance Philanthropy, UK Research and Innovation, and ARIA. In February 2026, OpenAI ($7.5M / £5.6M) and Microsoft joined as new funders, bringing total available funding to over £27 million (approximately $36.3 million). In its first round, grants ranging from £50,000 to £1,000,000 were awarded to over 60 projects across eight countries. The project offers three modes of support: grant funding, cloud compute resources (via AWS and others), and venture capital investment for alignment-focused startups. It is guided by an expert advisory board including Yoshua Bengio, Zico Kolter, Shafi Goldwasser, Buck Shlegeris, Andrea Lincoln, Marcelo Mattar, and Sydney Levine. A second funding round is expected to open in summer 2026.
entities.notes_sources: gov.uk; aisi.gov.uk; alignmentproject.aisi.gov.uk; www.govconexec.com; www.aisi.gov.uk; alignmentproject.aisi.gov.uk; lesserwrong.com; www.governmenttechnology.co.uk
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Alignment Project reflects a government-led but internationally collaborative stance: it does not advocate for broad AI prohibitions but actively funds technical safety research to ensure AI systems remain under human control and operate as intended. The UK government framing emphasizes that alignment research is a shared international responsibility and a precondition for safe deployment of advanced AI, suggesting a targeted, safety-first regulatory posture rather than a restrictive or laissez-faire one.
entities.belief_threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Power concentration

entities.name: The Eric and Wendy Schmidt Fund for Strategic Innovation
entities.notes: The Eric and Wendy Schmidt Fund for Strategic Innovation is a private philanthropic foundation (EIN: 46-3460261) established in September 2014 by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt and Wendy Schmidt, operating as the corporate foundation of Schmidt Futures. With approximately $1.4 billion in assets, it disbursed $254.7 million in charitable grants in fiscal year 2023 and $244.1 million across ~321 grantees in its 2024 filing year. The fund is the parent organization and 100% owner of the Special Competitive Studies Project (SCSP), founded in October 2021 to analyze and make recommendations to strengthen America's long-term competitiveness in AI and emerging technologies with national security implications; SCSP received $15.1–22.1 million from the fund in recent fiscal years. The foundation also owns Schmidt Sciences, LLC (a 501(c)3 advancing science and technology globally), Convergent Research (launching Focused Research Organizations to close R&D ecosystem gaps), and other initiatives including the Rise talent program for exceptional young people. Eric Schmidt serves as President and Director (uncompensated) and Wendy Schmidt as Director; the fund does not accept unsolicited grant applications and operates through pre-selected strategic decisions. Its grantmaking spans AI development, scientific research, national security competitiveness, and next-generation leadership in technology and geopolitics, with primary geographic focus on California, Massachusetts, and New York.
entities.notes_sources: en.wikipedia.org; littlesis.org; www.schmidtsciences.org; littlesis.org; www.hinchilla.com; impala.digital; littlesis.org; www.grantmakers.io
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The fund's primary policy vehicle, SCSP, is focused on strengthening U.S. competitiveness in AI and national security rather than advancing a specific regulatory framework. Eric Schmidt has publicly emphasized the need to maintain American leadership in AI against geopolitical rivals, suggesting a pro-development orientation, but SCSP also produces policy recommendations that include governance structures. No explicit regulatory stance (e.g., light-touch vs. moderate) is clearly articulated in available sources for the fund itself.
entities.belief_threat_models: National security, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality

entities.name: The MITRE Corporation
entities.notes: MITRE Corporation is a nonprofit, federally funded research and development organization (FFRDC) that works across national security, cybersecurity, and emerging technology domains. In December 2025, NIST announced a $20 million partnership with MITRE to establish two AI-focused research centers: the AI Economic Security Center for U.S. Manufacturing Productivity and the AI Economic Security Center to Secure U.S. Critical Infrastructure from Cyberthreats. MITRE has operated NIST's National Cybersecurity FFRDC (NCF) since 2014 and brings deep government-industry-academia collaboration capabilities to AI governance and applied research. The organization maintains several publicly available AI and cybersecurity tools including the ATT&CK, ATLAS, and CALDERA frameworks, as well as a state-of-the-art AI Lab and Federal AI Sandbox. The two new centers are designed to implement the White House's America's AI Action Plan and support NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI, formerly the U.S. AI Safety Institute). CEO Mark Peters emphasized MITRE's goal of producing 'transformational research to accelerate U.S. development and adoption of reliable, secure, and trustworthy AI.' MITRE's role as a trusted government partner positions it as a key institutional node in the U.S. federal AI policy and R&D ecosystem.
entities.notes_sources: www.mitre.org; www.manufacturingdive.com; www.cybersecuritydive.com; cyberscoop.com; www.infosecurity-magazine.com; kslaw.com; eamvision.com; www.qualitydigest.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: MITRE's work emphasizes developing AI safety standards, technical evaluations, and best practices rather than opposing or aggressively restricting AI. Its partnership with NIST focuses on 'reliable, secure, and trustworthy AI' and aligns with the America's AI Action Plan, suggesting support for targeted standards-based governance rather than heavy-handed regulation. Statements from CEO Mark Peters and NIST collaboration activities reflect a balanced approach that accelerates AI adoption while embedding assurance and security frameworks.
entities.belief_threat_models: Cybersecurity, National security, Bias/discrimination, Power concentration

entities.name: Thiel Foundation
entities.notes: The Thiel Foundation is a private philanthropic organization founded and chaired by Peter Thiel that supports science, technology, and long-term thinking about the future. Its flagship program, the Thiel Fellowship, was established in 2011 and awards grants to young entrepreneurs to pursue projects outside traditional educational tracks. The grant amount has grown over time — originally $100,000 per fellow, then $200,000, and as of the 2026 class, $250,000 over two years per fellow. The Foundation shapes the AI ecosystem by funding early-stage AI researchers and builders: notable AI-relevant alumni include Chris Olah (mechanistic interpretability researcher at Anthropic), Nicholas Jitkoff, and founders of Anthropic, Cognition, Etched, and other frontier AI companies. Over 300 fellows have been funded since inception, and their companies are collectively worth hundreds of billions of dollars. The Fellowship emphasizes investing in the individual rather than credentials, explicitly positioning itself as an alternative to conventional higher education. The 2026 class includes multiple fellows working on AI, robotics, and compute infrastructure.
entities.notes_sources: www.businesswire.com; www.morningstar.com; lasvegassun.com; vcwire.tech; thielfellowship.com; insights.tryspecter.com; jvq.net; www.financialcontent.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Peter Thiel, as Chairman, has publicly stated 'The Boomer career tracks no longer lead anywhere worth going,' signaling strong anti-establishment, anti-credentialist ideology. The Foundation's core model — paying young people to skip college and build companies without institutional gatekeeping — reflects a philosophy of minimal regulatory interference and acceleration of innovation outside traditional systems. Thiel has historically been a vocal critic of government overreach and regulatory barriers to technology.

entities.name: UK Research and Innovation (UKRI)
entities.notes: UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is the UK government's primary public funding body for research and innovation, operating under the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology. UKRI funds AI research across the UK through multiple councils, most notably EPSRC (Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council). It delivered the £20 million Turing AI Acceleration Fellowship programme, awarding 15 prestigious fellowships to leading UK AI researchers including Adrian Weller (Trustworthy Machine Learning at Cambridge/Alan Turing Institute) and others working on cancer AI and digital twins (announced November 2020). UKRI also funded the £7,446,261 AIRR DAWN Phase 1 project (March 2024–March 2028), a UK Industry-Academic co-design partnership delivering a pre-exascale research cloud for AI. The Turing AI Fellowships were delivered in partnership with the Department for Business Energy and Industrial Strategy, Office for AI, and The Alan Turing Institute. UKRI plays a central role in shaping the UK's AI research ecosystem by directing large-scale public investment toward trustworthy, responsible, and high-performance AI research, helping sustain the UK's international competitiveness in AI.
entities.notes_sources: www.lcfi.ac.uk; www.turing.ac.uk; www.lcfi.ac.uk; www.turing.ac.uk; ai.cam.ac.uk; news.europawire.eu; www.lcfi.ac.uk; www.bennettinstitute.cam.ac.uk
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: UKRI's funding priorities explicitly balance AI innovation with ethical imperatives, directing investment toward trustworthy, fair, interpretable, and robust AI systems. Its fellowship programmes target responsible AI research in high-stakes domains like healthcare and criminal justice, reflecting a moderate stance that supports targeted governance of AI risks without opposing development.
entities.belief_threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Loss of control, National security

entities.name: U.S. Air Force
entities.notes: The U.S. Air Force is a branch of the U.S. Armed Forces responsible for aerial warfare, air defense, and strategic deterrence. It shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through large-scale R&D funding and partnerships with leading research universities and defense contractors. As evidenced by the edge context, the Air Force funds MIT at approximately $15 million per year, building on a five-decade relationship. Its FY2027 budget request of $267.7 billion (a 38% increase over FY2026) includes a 50% increase in Research, Development, Test and Evaluation spending to $98.2 billion, directly funding AI-adjacent programs such as Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCAs)—semi-autonomous drone wingmen—with $2.37 billion in FY2027 alone. The Air Force is advancing autonomous and semi-autonomous systems including the YFQ-42 (General Atomics) and YFQ-44 (Anduril) CCA platforms, representing a major investment in AI-enabled military autonomy. The service also funds sixth-generation fighter development (F-47) and next-generation autonomous systems at scale, making it one of the largest funders of AI and autonomous systems research in the U.S. government. Air Force Chief of Staff Gen. Ken Wilsbach has emphasized readiness and modernization as dual priorities, with autonomous systems central to future warfighting doctrine.
entities.notes_sources: www.stripes.com; www.airandspaceforces.com; www.airandspaceforces.com; www.airforcemag.com; www.airandspaceforces.com; www.airandspaceforces.com; www.airforcetimes.com; breakingdefense.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Air Force operates under DoD AI ethics guidelines and existing acquisition regulations, but as a military service branch rather than a regulatory body, it does not articulate a public stance on civilian AI regulation. Its massive investment in autonomous weapons systems (CCAs, F-47) suggests a strong 'accelerate' posture for military AI, while DoD-wide policies impose internal governance requirements. No explicit public statements on civilian AI regulatory stance are present in the available sources.

entities.name: VAST Data
entities.notes: VAST Data is a New York-based AI infrastructure company founded in 2016 that develops software for storing and managing large amounts of data for AI applications, positioning itself as the 'AI Operating System' at the center of the AI ecosystem. The company supports AI environments running on millions of GPUs worldwide, with notable customers including CoreWeave, Mistral AI, Cursor, Lowe's, and the U.S. Air Force. In April 2026, VAST Data closed a Series F funding round led by Drive Capital and Access Industries at a $30 billion valuation — more than triple its $9.1 billion Series E valuation from late 2023 — raising approximately $1 billion in primary and secondary capital with participation from NVIDIA, Fidelity Management & Research Company, and NEA. The company has surpassed $4 billion in cumulative bookings and exited its last fiscal year with more than $500 million in committed annual recurring revenue. VAST's core innovation is a storage architecture designed from the deep learning era to address trade-offs among scale, simplicity, performance, and cost, competing with NetApp, Pure Storage, Dell, AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure. The company employs approximately 1,200 people and plans to use new funds for global expansion, strategic acquisitions, and partnership development. NVIDIA's participation is strategically significant given its central role in AI computing infrastructure.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public policy positions on AI regulation are found in the sources. The company's framing around 'accelerating demand for artificial generally intelligent systems' and its infrastructure-focused mission suggest alignment with industry-led, growth-oriented approaches, but no direct regulatory stance has been stated.

entities.name: AMP PBC
entities.notes: AMP PBC is a newly formed venture firm founded by Anjney Midha, a former general partner at Andreessen Horowitz (a16z). Midha secured $1.3 billion for AMP's first fund, making it a notable debut in the venture capital landscape. AMP PBC is listed as a significant investor in Anthropic's $30 billion Series G funding round (announced February 2026), which valued Anthropic at $380 billion post-money, confirming a reported $300 million investment in Anthropic as part of that round. The firm shapes the AI ecosystem directly through large-scale capital allocation to frontier AI labs. Midha's background at a16z, one of the most influential technology venture firms, brings deep networks and expertise to AMP. The 'PBC' designation (Public Benefit Corporation) may signal an intent to balance profit with social mission, though details are limited in available sources. AMP represents a new generation of AI-focused venture capital emerging from established firm alumni.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation from AMP PBC or Anjney Midha are present in the available sources. As a major investor in Anthropic — a company that publicly advocates for targeted AI safety regulation — AMP's investment choices may suggest alignment with moderate or targeted regulatory stances, but this is inferred from association rather than explicit statements.

entities.name: Andrew W. Mellon Foundation
entities.notes: The Andrew W. Mellon Foundation is one of the largest private foundations in the United States, with assets historically around $4 billion or more, focused primarily on higher education, arts, humanities, and public knowledge. It shapes the AI ecosystem through targeted philanthropic grants at the intersection of AI and humanistic inquiry. In March 2023, it awarded Emory University $1.3 million for the Atlanta Interdisciplinary AI (AIAI) Network, a collaboration involving Emory, Clark Atlanta University, Georgia Institute of Technology, and the DataedX Group — directly funding AI-related research and education. The Foundation has a long history of grants to Emory and partner institutions including a $1.25 million grant in 2019 for Humanities Pathways and a $2.4 million grant for Indigenous studies. Its AI-related funding tends to focus on humanistic, ethical, and interdisciplinary approaches to AI rather than technical development. It operates as a selective grantmaker and does not pursue commercial or government revenue. Its engagement in AI is primarily through funding academic institutions that explore the social, ethical, and educational dimensions of AI.

entities.name: Capital K (KAUST Deep Tech Innovation Fund)
entities.location: Silicon Valley, California, USA (operating); Thuwal, Saudi Arabia (affiliated with KAUST)
entities.notes: Capital K is a ~$200 million (SAR 750 million) deep tech venture capital fund launched in August 2023 by Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman as part of KAUST's 'Accelerating Impact' strategy. It is based in Silicon Valley and operates independently of KAUST, though it is funded by the university. The fund aims to invest $2–5 million into 20–25 early-stage deep tech projects over five to ten years, targeting companies from KAUST, Saudi Arabia, and internationally that contribute to the Kingdom's economic development and job creation goals under Vision 2030. Capital K is one of three major initiatives in KAUST's new strategy, alongside the National Transformation Institute for Applied Research (NTI) and restructured research centers. It prioritizes sectors including health and wellness, sustainable environment, energy, and 'economies of the future,' which explicitly encompass AI and advanced technology. The fund is designed to help KAUST-developed IP reach the market and to position Saudi Arabia as a hub for foreign investment and deep tech, including AI-related startups. Its Silicon Valley base and focus on global deep tech investment place it directly within the AI funding ecosystem.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Capital K and the broader KAUST strategy are oriented toward accelerating commercialization and deep tech investment with no stated regulatory constraints. The fund is designed to operate independently and flexibly, focused on growth and economic diversification rather than imposing governance requirements on AI development.
entities.belief_threat_models: National security, Economic inequality, Labor displacement

entities.name: DAF-MIT AI Accelerator
entities.notes: The DAF-MIT AI Accelerator is a cooperative agreement between the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and the Department of the Air Force (DAF), established in May 2019 with an initial investment of approximately $15 million per year from the Air Force. The program is designed to conduct fundamental AI research enabling rapid prototyping, scaling, and ethical application of AI algorithms and systems for both defense and broader societal benefit. In its first phase (2020–2024), it launched 10 interdisciplinary research projects spanning 15 workstreams, engaging over 140 faculty, researchers, and students from more than 20 MIT organizational units, with embedded Airmen serving as liaisons to DoD stakeholders. Research areas include weather modeling, optimization of training schedules, autonomy, disaster response, and medical readiness, resulting in nearly 500 published papers cited over 8,000 times. In 2024, the DAF and MIT renewed their cooperative agreement for another five years ('AIA 2.0'), with a ribbon-cutting ceremony attended by Air Force Chief of Staff Gen. David Allvin and MIT CSAIL Director Professor Daniela Rus. The accelerator is housed within MIT's Beaver Works facility in Kendall Square and is a component of the MIT Stephen A. Schwarzman College of Computing. It has served as a funder of external AI research projects, including MIT FutureTech, which studies economic and technical trends in computing and AI.
entities.notes_sources: aia.mit.edu; news.mit.edu; futuretech.mit.edu; www.aiaccelerator.af.mil; aia.mit.edu; futuretech.mit.edu; www.aiaccelerator.af.mil; futuretech.mit.edu
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The accelerator explicitly emphasizes the 'ethical use of AI technologies for society and the nation' and describes its mission as enabling rapid prototyping while ensuring ethical application of AI systems. Its mandate balances acceleration of AI capabilities with ethical guardrails, reflecting a moderate, targeted approach rather than either a permissive or restrictive extreme.

entities.name: Draper Laboratory
entities.notes: Draper Laboratory is a nonprofit research, development, and manufacturing company headquartered in Cambridge, Massachusetts, with more than 2,500 employees across 12 locations. The organization focuses on four critical mission areas: Strategic Systems, Space Systems, Electronic Systems, and Biotechnology Systems. In February 2026, Draper received an up to $26.7 million Other Transaction Award (OTA) from ARPA-H to support the CATALYST program, developing in silico models of human physiology using AI/ML for drug development, including its PREDICT96 microphysiological system. Draper also holds a $26 million Pentagon OTA contract awarded in January 2024 to develop autonomous UAS software for CBRN reconnaissance missions using AI-driven sensor-fusion and autonomous teaming algorithms. In March 2026, Draper joined the ARPA-H BREATHE-funded SCALE team to develop advanced biosensors and AI-driven respiratory risk assessment software for building air quality. Draper shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying AI/ML systems for defense autonomy, drug development modeling, and public health, primarily as a federally funded R&D contractor. The organization also runs the Draper Scholars program and DraperSPARX to engage startups and the next generation of innovators.
entities.notes_sources: www.prnewswire.com; www.draper.com; www.draper.com; militaryembedded.com; www.prweb.com; www.dawnbreaker.com; www.prnewswire.com; finance.yahoo.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulatory stance were found in the sources. Draper operates primarily as a government contractor aligned with federal R&D objectives; its work on AI/ML for drug development explicitly supports FDA IND filing pathways and ARPA-H programmatic goals, suggesting acceptance of regulatory frameworks rather than opposition or advocacy for specific stances.

entities.name: Emergent Ventures
entities.notes: Emergent Ventures is a fellowship and grant program housed at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University, launched in 2018 with a $1 million seed grant from the Thiel Foundation and administered by economist and Mercatus faculty director Tyler Cowen. The program funds 'zero to one' ideas from entrepreneurs, researchers, and thinkers worldwide, awarding grants ranging from a few hundred dollars to tens of thousands. It has supported numerous individuals relevant to the AI policy and safety ecosystem, including Leopold Aschenbrenner (AI safety/governance researcher), Marius Hobbhahn (AI safety researcher), Dwarkesh Patel (AI-focused journalist/podcaster), and Sam Bowman (now on Mercatus board). The program has expanded to include geographic verticals for India, Ukraine, and Africa & the Caribbean, with the latter supported by Schmidt Futures. A COVID-focused spin-off, Fast Grants, raised over $50 million and awarded 260 grants for COVID-19 research starting in April 2020. Emergent Ventures shapes the AI ecosystem primarily as a talent-discovery and early-stage funding mechanism for researchers and communicators working on AI safety, progress studies, and adjacent intellectual fields.
entities.notes_sources: www.mercatus.org; mercatus.org; www.mercatus.org; www.mercatus.org; newscience.org; www.cato.org; www.mercatus.org; marginalrevolution.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Emergent Ventures is housed at the Mercatus Center, a libertarian-leaning think tank with a strong preference for market-based solutions and limited government intervention. The program's mission emphasizes entrepreneurialism, decentralized innovation, and 'classical liberal' insights, and its founding was supported by the Thiel Foundation. Grantees span AI safety research to charter cities, reflecting a disposition toward bottom-up innovation over regulatory mandates.
entities.belief_threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

entities.name: Fundamental AI Research Lab (FAIR Laboratory)
entities.location: United Kingdom (host institution to be selected)
entities.notes: The Fundamental AI Research Lab (FAIR Laboratory) was announced on March 4, 2026 by the UK's Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) and UK Research and Innovation (UKRI), backed by up to £40 million over 6 years plus substantial in-kind access to AI Research Resource compute capacity worth tens of millions of pounds. The lab is designed to support bold, high-risk fundamental AI research that addresses structural limitations in current AI systems — including hallucinations, unreliable memory, and unpredictable reasoning — rather than simply scaling existing models. Applications are administered through EPSRC (Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council), and proposals are assessed by a peer review panel chaired by Raia Hadsell, VP of Research at Google DeepMind and a DSIT AI Ambassador. The lab is explicitly intended to pursue research not currently pursued by commercial AI labs due to its early-stage or high-risk nature, positioning the UK as a global leader in fundamental AI development. It forms an early deliverable of UKRI's first-ever AI Strategy, which commits £1.6 billion over four years to AI research, infrastructure, and skills. The host institution will be selected through a competitive process open to consortia with world-leading track records in fundamental AI research. The lab's goals include strengthening the UK talent pipeline, attracting international researchers, enabling open-source translation of research, and growing sovereign AI capability.
entities.notes_sources: www.ukri.org; www.gov.uk; www.lasr.ac.uk; www.linkedin.com; www.aisi.gov.uk; geodesicresearch.org; www.linkedin.com; syntelesis.co.uk
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The lab's mandate emphasizes trustworthy, transparent, and reliable AI development and explicitly aims to ensure 'UK values are baked in from the outset' (AI Minister Kanishka Narayan, March 2026). It supports open-sourcing of research and industry translation, suggesting a balanced approach that encourages responsible development without imposing broad restrictions.
entities.belief_threat_models: Misinformation, Cybersecurity, National security, Loss of control

entities.name: German Federal Government (Bund)
entities.notes: The German Federal Government ('Bund') is the central government of the Federal Republic of Germany, acting as a major funder and policy actor in science, research, and digitalization. As confirmed in Source [4], the Bund co-funds the Weizenbaum-Institut für die vernetzte Gesellschaft—Germany's premier internet and digitalization research institute—alongside the states of Berlin and Brandenburg, with a new institutional funding agreement effective from October 2027. The Weizenbaum Institute, operating under the mandate of the Federal Ministry for Research (Bundesforschungsministerium), investigates societal impacts of digitalization and AI. The Bund also participates in numerous Bund-Länder agreements through the Gemeinsame Wissenschaftskonferenz (GWK), coordinating large-scale research funding including for AI-adjacent institutions. The federal government's involvement in funding AI and digitalization research positions it as a key shaper of Germany's AI policy ecosystem. The Wissenschaftsrat's 2024 evaluation of the Weizenbaum Institute praised its high scientific quality and recommended permanent institutional funding, which the Bund has moved to implement. Germany's federal government also sets overarching AI strategy and participates in EU-level AI regulation deliberations.
entities.notes_sources: www.berlin.de; www.berlin.de; www.senatspressestelle.bremen.de; www.berlin.de; www.senatspressestelle.bremen.de; www.gesundheit.bremen.de; www.senatspressestelle.bremen.de; www.finanzen.bremen.de
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The German Federal Government supports structured, institutionalized oversight of AI and digitalization through funding of research bodies like the Weizenbaum Institute, which advises policymakers on AI impacts. Germany has been an active participant in EU AI Act negotiations, generally supporting a risk-based regulatory framework—consistent with a moderate, balanced stance. The Bund's co-funding of interdisciplinary research on societal AI impacts signals a preference for evidence-based governance rather than either laissez-faire or precautionary approaches.
entities.belief_threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Democratic erosion, Labor displacement, Power concentration

entities.name: Government of Quebec
entities.notes: The Government of Quebec is the provincial government of Quebec, Canada, and plays a direct role in shaping the AI ecosystem through targeted funding and strategic investment in AI research infrastructure. It provided CAD $36 million in funding to Mila (the Montreal Institute for Learning Algorithms), one of the world's leading AI research centers, linking research capacity and talent development to the province's economic ambitions. Quebec Minister for the Economy Christopher Skeete was involved in a $19.35 million funding commitment to McGill University, further supporting AI-adjacent research and talent pipelines. The government is also a backer of Inovia Capital, a major Canadian venture fund, through its investing arm, contributing to a $450 million fund that supports AI and tech startups. The Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec (CDPQ), a public institutional investor created by and accountable to the Quebec government in 1965, manages over $517 billion in assets and is a key vehicle for the province's economic development investments. Quebec has positioned itself as a global hub for AI research and talent, with Montreal serving as home to Mila, making the provincial government a meaningful non-U.S. government actor in the global AI ecosystem.
entities.notes_sources: www.cdpq.com; www.lacaisse.com; www.cdpq.com; www.cdpq.com; thecanadianencyclopedia.ca; www.cgaa.org; www.cdpq.com; www.cdpq.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Quebec's government has taken a supportive, enabling stance toward AI by investing heavily in research institutions like Mila and universities, suggesting a balanced approach that promotes development while maintaining public oversight through institutions like CDPQ. No explicit restrictive AI regulation positions are evident from the sources, but the government retains a public interest guardian role in its investment frameworks.

entities.name: HSBC Strategic Innovation Investments
entities.notes: HSBC is one of the world's largest banking and financial services organizations, headquartered in London, UK. Its corporate venture capital arm, HSBC Strategic Innovation Investments, has been active since 2014, making minority investments in early-stage technology companies with strategic relevance to HSBC's core banking business. As of 2024, HSBC has invested in 21 companies across Enterprise Applications, FinTech, and High Tech sectors, with a portfolio that includes 4 unicorns and 7 acquisitions. Notable AI-relevant investments include Quantexa (contextual decision intelligence/AI, Series C 2020 and Series E 2023), Contextual AI (Series A 2024), Elliptic (blockchain analytics/AI, 2025), Axoni (distributed ledger, Series B), and OpenFin (financial OS, Series C 2019). HSBC is simultaneously a customer of several AI companies it invests in, including Quantexa and Elliptic, deepening strategic alignment. The firm's investment thesis centers on financial crime detection, data intelligence, cybersecurity, and financial infrastructure — areas where AI is central. Richard May, HSBC's Group Head of Financial Crime, joined Elliptic's board following their 2025 investment, reflecting active engagement beyond passive capital.
entities.notes_sources: www.uktechnews.info; www.finsmes.com; businesscloud.co.uk; www.einpresswire.com; tracxn.com; www.globenewswire.com; www.elliptic.co; news.fintech.io
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: HSBC's investment in Elliptic — described explicitly as helping 'meet rising regulatory expectations and industry standards' around digital assets and financial crime — signals support for targeted compliance-oriented AI regulation. As a globally systemically important bank, HSBC operates under extensive financial regulation and appears to favor AI governance frameworks that support risk management and compliance rather than opposing regulation.

entities.name: Imbue
entities.notes: Imbue (formerly known as Generally Intelligent) is a San Francisco-based AI startup focused on building practical AI systems that can reason and accomplish large-scale, real-world tasks, with a particular emphasis on autonomous AI agents and coding capabilities. The company rebranded from 'Generally Intelligent' to 'Imbue' in September 2023 alongside its $200M Series B announcement, valuing the company at over $1 billion. Its Series B was led by the Astera Institute with participation from NVIDIA, Cruise CEO Kyle Vogt, Notion co-founder Simon Last, GPT-3 author Tom Brown, and Dropbox CEO Drew Houston. In October 2023, the company raised an additional $12 million from Amazon's Alexa Fund and former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, bringing total Series B funding to over $210 million. Total funding across all rounds stands at approximately $232 million. CEO Kanjun Qiu has articulated the company's vision as 'reinventing the personal computer so that it's truly personal' by building AI agents that work reliably and have strong reasoning capabilities. Imbue sits in a select group of well-capitalized AI labs capable of developing foundation models, and its work on reasoning and autonomous agents directly shapes frontier AI development.
entities.notes_sources: imbue.com; tracxn.com; www.reuters.com; www.crunchbase.com; www.imbue.com; www.gaebler.com; www.usnews.com; www.maginative.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory policy statements found in sources. Imbue's focus on building 'trustworthy' and 'robust' agents suggests some self-governance orientation. CEO Kanjun Qiu's public statements emphasize reliability and practicality of AI agents rather than regulatory frameworks, suggesting a builder-first, light-touch stance inferred from company positioning.

entities.name: Index Ventures
entities.location: London, UK and San Francisco, CA
entities.notes: Index Ventures is a multi-stage venture capital firm with headquarters in London and San Francisco, founded to support ambitious entrepreneurs globally. The firm has been an active investor in AI and enterprise technology companies. Index Ventures led Cohere's $40 million Series A round in September 2021, with partner Mike Volpi joining Cohere's board as part of the investment. Index Ventures continued as an existing investor in Cohere's $270 million Series C round in June 2023, led by Inovia Capital. The firm also co-led Ideogram's $16.5 million seed financing round alongside Andreessen Horowitz, backing the Toronto-based generative AI image startup. Mike Volpi, co-founder and partner at Index Ventures, has been a key figure in the firm's AI investments and has publicly championed democratizing access to NLP and other AI technologies. Through its investments in enterprise AI infrastructure and generative AI startups, Index Ventures plays a meaningful role in shaping the development and scaling of foundational AI technologies.

Inferred from investment behavior: Index Ventures has backed companies focused on broad AI accessibility and democratization (Cohere, Ideogram), suggesting a preference for enabling rapid AI development with minimal regulatory friction. Mike Volpi's framing of Cohere's mission as 'democratizing access to one of the most important technologies of our time' implies an accelerationist, light-touch regulatory disposition.

entities.name: Innovate UK
entities.notes: Innovate UK is the UK government's national innovation agency, operating as part of UK Research and Innovation (UKRI), tasked with supporting UK businesses in developing and commercializing new products, processes, and services. It administers the Smart Grant program, a highly competitive bi-annual scheme offering shares of up to £25 million in government R&D funding; only 2% of applicants across multiple sectors receive awards, with roughly 46 companies selected per cycle from nearly 2,000 applications. Innovate UK actively funds AI-driven technology companies, as evidenced by its £500,000 Smart Grant to Electric Sheep in 2024 for the development of Spotlight V3, an AI-powered rotoscoping tool for the VFX industry. The agency's mandate is to identify and back 'game changing and world leading ideas,' explicitly including AI applications in creative industries, making it a direct funder and enabler of the UK AI ecosystem. Its Smart Grants program is administered through Innovate UK Business Connect. By channeling government funds into AI startups and R&D projects, Innovate UK plays a significant role in shaping the UK's competitive position in AI development and deployment.
entities.notes_sources: broadcastnow.co.uk; startupsmagazine.co.uk; televisual.com; lookai.vc; lookai.vc; en.ain.ua; www.vestbee.com; www.vestbee.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: As a UK government innovation agency, Innovate UK operates within the UK's broader AI regulatory framework, which favors a pro-innovation, context-specific approach rather than blanket regulation. Its grant-making activity reflects a belief that government should actively support AI development while ensuring it benefits the economy and society, consistent with the UK government's AI policy stance of enabling innovation with targeted oversight.

entities.name: Institute for Law & AI (LawAI)
entities.location: Boston, MA (with presence in Washington DC, Cambridge UK)
entities.notes: The Institute for Law & AI (LawAI), formerly known as the Legal Priorities Project, is an independent 501(c)(3) nonprofit think tank founded in 2020 that researches and advises on the legal challenges posed by artificial intelligence. Headquartered in Boston, MA, LawAI conducts research at the intersection of AI, law, and policy, and advises governments, international organizations, public institutions, and private actors. The organization runs annual Summer Research Fellowships (US and EU tracks) offering paid positions to law students, professionals, and academics to work on AI law and policy questions, with the 2024 fellowship funded in part by a $75,000 grant from the Good Ventures Foundation. LawAI reported total revenues of approximately $7.85 million for FY2024, a dramatic increase from $963K in FY2023, with major funders including the National Philanthropic Trust ($4.97M) and Vanguard Charitable Endowment Program ($245K). The organization employs approximately 40 staff as of early 2026 and operates internationally across 12 countries. Key personnel include Cullen O'Keefe (Chief of Staff), MacKenzie Arnold (Director of US Policy), and Eric Martinez (Board Director and Treasurer). LawAI's work focuses on preparing legal and governance frameworks to address risks from transformative AI, including hosting a Summer School on Law-Following AI and publishing research on agentic AI systems and other frontier legal challenges.
entities.notes_sources: www.goodventures.org; law-ai.org; law-ai.org; law-ai.org; law-ai.org; www.openphilanthropy.org; www.causeiq.com; ea.greaterwrong.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: LawAI's mission centers on developing 'sound legal analysis' to 'promote security, welfare, and the rule of law' in response to AI challenges. Their research and advisory work with governments and international organizations suggests a moderate, rule-of-law-grounded approach to AI regulation rather than either accelerationist or precautionary extremes. Their focus on 'law-following AI' and legal frameworks implies support for targeted legal standards without necessarily advocating for broad prohibitions.
entities.belief_threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, National security, Democratic erosion

entities.name: Institute for New Economic Thinking (INET)
entities.notes: The Institute for New Economic Thinking (INET) is a nonprofit think tank and grant-making organization founded to challenge conventional economic orthodoxy and advance heterodox economic research. INET has funded influential AI-related research, notably supporting Joseph Stiglitz (Columbia University) and Anton Korinek (University of Virginia) on the economic implications of artificial intelligence, including their 2017 NBER working paper 'Artificial Intelligence and Its Implications for Income Distribution and Unemployment' and subsequent papers through 2025. INET's research in this area focuses heavily on how AI-driven labor displacement, technological unemployment, and winner-takes-all dynamics could exacerbate inequality and poverty, particularly in developing economies. The organization runs a Center for Innovation, Growth and Society (INET-CIGS), which specifically funds AI economics research, and has co-hosted events such as the INET/IMF Conference on Macroeconomics in the Age of AI. INET publishes working papers through its own working paper series (e.g., WP No. 146, 232) and disseminates research through partnerships with NBER and SSRN. It also supports the Young Scholars Initiative (YSI) and the Global Commission on Economic Transformation, which engages Stiglitz and others on structural economic reform including technology policy.
entities.notes_sources: www.ineteconomics.org; www.ineteconomics.org; www.ineteconomics.org; www.nber.org; papers.ssrn.com; conference.nber.org; www.ineteconomics.org; www.ineteconomics.org
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: INET-funded research by Stiglitz and Korinek explicitly argues that without 'radical reform of tax and redistribution politics,' AI will produce widespread technological unemployment and poverty (NY Times citation, 2021). Their research calls for substantial government intervention, redistribution reform, and reforms to global economic governance to counteract AI's adverse effects on labor and developing economies.
entities.belief_threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

entities.name: Jakob N. Foerster
entities.title: Associate Professor, Department of Engineering Science, University of Oxford; Research Scientist, FAIR (Meta AI); Director of Foerster Lab for AI Research (FLAIR)
entities.other_orgs: Meta AI (FAIR), Cooperative AI Foundation (grant recipient), CIFAR (AI Chair), St. Anne's College Oxford
entities.notes: Jakob N. Foerster is an Associate Professor in the Department of Engineering Science at the University of Oxford, where he joined in fall 2021, and simultaneously holds a Research Scientist position at Meta AI's Fundamental AI Research (FAIR) lab, splitting time 50/50 between the two. He is the director of the Foerster Lab for AI Research (FLAIR) at Oxford. During his PhD at Oxford, he helped pioneer deep multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), with internships at Google Brain, OpenAI, and DeepMind. He was the lead organizer of the first Emergent Communication workshop at NeurIPS in 2017 and was awarded a prestigious CIFAR AI Chair in 2019. He received a Cooperative AI Foundation grant (GBP 166,370, 2021–2023) alongside Christian Schroeder de Witt to support the FLAIR lab, and was awarded a Horizon Europe Guarantee grant (£1,905,276, Jan 2024–Dec 2028) for 'Scalable Human-AI Coordination from First Principles.' In 2024, his lab received the 2023 Amazon Research Award ($70,000 cash + $50,000 AWS credit) for work on compute-only scaling of large language models. His research spans multi-agent RL, zero-shot coordination, cooperative AI, and emergent communication, making him one of the most prominent academic researchers in cooperative and multi-agent AI.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation were found in the sources. His research focus on cooperative AI and human-AI coordination suggests awareness of alignment and safety concerns, but no direct policy positions are documented.

entities.name: John Arnold
entities.title: Co-founder of Arnold Ventures; Philanthropist; Vanderbilt University Trustee; Former Energy Trader
entities.other_orgs: Vanderbilt University Board of Trust
entities.notes: John Arnold (BA '95, Vanderbilt) is a billionaire philanthropist and co-founder of Arnold Ventures, a major philanthropic organization he leads with his wife Laura Arnold (Harvard BA, Yale JD). He is a former natural gas trader at Enron who became one of the most successful hedge fund managers before retiring to focus on philanthropy. In March 2025, Arnold and Laura committed $25 million to Vanderbilt University's newly established College of Connected Computing—the university's first new college in 40 years—to fund endowed chairs including the inaugural dean's chair and three faculty chairs, as well as computing education and research initiatives in AI, data science, and interdisciplinary computing. Arnold and Laura are Giving Pledge signatories and have given over $34.5 million to Vanderbilt overall, including the Arnold Family Scholarship, Arnold Scholars Program, and the Laura and John Arnold Fund supporting public policy and quantitative analytics programs. Arnold served on Vanderbilt's Board of Trust from 2010–2014 and was reelected in 2023. His funding of a computing college explicitly focused on AI and data science positions him as a meaningful funder in the AI education and research ecosystem.

entities.name: Jump Trading Group
entities.notes: Jump Trading Group is a leading data and research-driven quantitative trading firm founded in 1999, headquartered in Chicago with 13 offices across 8 countries and approximately 1,396 employees. The firm uses AI and machine learning as core components of its trading operations, building advanced models, low-latency infrastructure, custom foundation models, and LLM agents integrated across its tools and data. In 2025, Jump Trading became one of the first financial services firms to deploy the NVIDIA Vera Rubin Platform in a deep learning financial research data center expansion, signaling significant investment in AI compute infrastructure. Jump Trading actively funds external AI and neuroscience research: in 2025 it established Jump Trading Fellowships at the University of Chicago, offering PhD candidates full tuition, fees, and ~$50,000 stipends to advance scientific research across six fields. In 2026, it partnered with UCL's Gatsby Computational Neuroscience Unit to fund four fully supported PhD scholarships beginning autumn 2026, covering tuition, stipend, travel, and visa costs, to support research at the intersection of neuroscience, machine learning, and AI. The firm's CTO Alex Davies has publicly articulated Jump Trading's commitment to advancing transformative research at the neuroscience-AI interface. Jump Trading shapes the AI ecosystem through both its internal deployment of frontier AI/ML systems in high-stakes trading environments and its philanthropic funding of academic AI and computational neuroscience research.

entities.name: KAIST
entities.notes: KAIST (Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology) is South Korea's premier public research university, specializing in science, engineering, and technology. The institution has a Graduate School of AI, reflecting its central role in AI research and education in South Korea. KAIST alumni have made significant contributions to global AI research, including Professor Kyunghyun Cho (BS 2009, School of Computing), who became a prominent AI researcher at NYU known for foundational work on neural machine translation and attention mechanisms. KAIST received scholarship endowments from Cho in 2019 ($3,000 to support female AI PhD students attending ICLR) and again in 2021 (100 million KRW Lim Mi-Sook Scholarship for female computer scientists, funded from his Samsung Ho-Am Prize money). The university has actively engaged with diversity and inclusion in AI/STEM fields. As a leading Asian academic institution producing influential AI researchers, KAIST shapes the global AI ecosystem through research output, talent development, and international collaborations.

entities.name: Kim Jungsik (Kim Jeong-sik)
entities.title: Founder and Chairman of Daeduck Electronics; Founder of Haedong Science and Culture Foundation; Major philanthropist supporting AI research at Seoul National University
entities.other_orgs: Haedong Science and Culture Foundation, Daeduck Welfare Foundation, Seoul National University (alumnus)
entities.notes: Kim Jungsik (1929–2019) was the founder and chairman of Daeduck Electronics, one of Korea's pioneering printed circuit board (PCB) manufacturers, which grew to roughly 2,000 employees and 96 billion won in annual revenue. He founded the company in 1965 (some sources say 1972) and built it into a key supplier to Samsung Electronics. In February 2019, while hospitalized due to advanced age, he made a landmark 50 billion won (~$37.5M USD) donation to his alma mater Seoul National University to establish an interdisciplinary AI research center modeled on MIT's new College of Computing — reportedly inspired by a New Year's Day news article about Stephen Schwarzman's 300 billion won donation to MIT. His total cumulative donations to SNU reached 65.7 billion won, the largest ever from a single individual to that institution. In 1991, he founded the Haedong Science and Culture Foundation, which funded 282 science and engineering researchers, provided scholarships to approximately 280 students, built libraries at over 20 engineering colleges nationwide, and co-published 70 academic books with the National Academy of Engineering of Korea. He passed away on April 11, 2019, at age 90, and was posthumously recognized as a science and technology meritorious person by the Korean government.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No direct statements on AI regulation were found in sources. His actions suggest strong support for accelerating AI research and infrastructure investment in Korea, driven by competitive concern about falling behind the U.S. and Japan, but he expressed no explicit regulatory position.

entities.name: Kitware, Inc.
entities.notes: Kitware, Inc. is a research and software development company founded in 1998, widely recognized for its contributions to open-source software including the Visualization Toolkit (VTK), Insight Segmentation and Registration Toolkit (ITK), CMake, and ParaView. The company has a strong focus on computer vision, medical imaging, scientific visualization, and AI/ML research, and has been a major recipient of DARPA and AFRL funding. In 2010, Kitware received an $11 million DARPA contract to lead Phase II of the VIRAT (Video and Image Retrieval and Analysis Tool) program, assembling a large team including Honeywell, Raytheon BBN, BAE Systems, General Dynamics, Lockheed Martin, and numerous top research universities. The company also received DARPA funding for the PerSEAS program for wide-area motion imagery analysis, with total DARPA computer vision contract wins exceeding $30 million by around 2011. Kitware's computer vision team, which grew to over 30 members, has worked on large-scale behavior recognition, object detection and tracking, activity recognition, and anomaly detection. Notable alumni include Matt Turek, who led Kitware's computer vision team before joining DARPA in 2018 as a Program Manager in the Information Innovation Office (I2O), where he managed programs including Explainable AI (XAI), Media Forensics (MediFor), Semantic Forensics (SemaFor), and Machine Common Sense (MCS). Kitware's partnerships extend to NIH, U.S. National Labs, Harvard, MIT, and Stanford, as well as commercial sectors including medical, pharmaceutical, and engineering simulation.

Kitware's work primarily involves building government-contracted AI and computer vision systems for DARPA and AFRL, suggesting alignment with U.S. national security priorities, but no formal regulatory stance is articulated.

entities.name: Laidir Foundation
entities.notes: Laidir Foundation is a private, non-operating philanthropic foundation based in Seattle, WA (EIN: 47-2519767), tax-exempt since April 2015 and directed by Brian Lonergan (President & Treasurer) and Matthew McBay (Vice President). With approximately $81M in assets as of 2024, it distributed roughly $4M annually across 22+ grantees. The foundation is a significant funder in the effective altruism and AI safety ecosystem: in 2023 it gave $500,000 to the AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund (via Vanguard Charitable), $300,000 to The Future Society to improve AI governance, $540,000 to Effective Ventures Foundation USA (including Longview Philanthropy and 80,000 Hours support), and $150,000 to the Effective Institutions Project. In 2024, it gave $390,000 to the Effective Institutions Project, $500,000 to Princeton University for the Craig Samuel Falls AI Policy Fellowship, and continued multi-year support for 80,000 Hours (totaling £400,000–£1M across years including 2024, 2022, 2019, 2018, 2017, and pre-2017). Beyond AI, it also funds housing policy (Sightline Institute), global poverty (Innovations for Poverty Action), and congressional reform (Rebuild Congress Initiative/Issue One). The foundation only makes contributions to preselected organizations and does not accept unsolicited applications.
