entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public regulatory stance has been articulated by NFDG as an entity. However, their lead investment in SSI — a company explicitly focused on safe superintelligence — suggests some alignment with safety-conscious AI development rather than pure acceleration.

entities.name: Nicolas Berggruen Charitable Trust
entities.notes: The Nicolas Berggruen Charitable Trust is the philanthropic vehicle of investor and philanthropist Nicolas Berggruen, serving as the primary funder of the Berggruen Institute. In 2016, the Trust made a landmark $500 million endowment grant to the Berggruen Institute on its fifth anniversary, enabling the institute to expand its global mission and build a permanent Los Angeles headquarters. The Trust is administered through Berggruen Holdings, which functions as the investment vehicle of the Trust. As of 2024, the Berggruen Institute's endowment has grown to approximately $1 billion. Nicolas Berggruen is a signatory of The Giving Pledge, reflecting his commitment to dedicating the majority of his wealth to philanthropy. The Trust's support of the Berggruen Institute directly shapes the AI ecosystem through the Institute's research programs on science, technology, synthetic intelligence, and the governance of transformative technologies. The Institute's work explicitly addresses artificial intelligence through its Futurology podcast, planetary governance frameworks, and research themes bridging philosophy, technology, and policy.
entities.notes_sources: www.businesswire.com; www.berggruen.org; www.berggruen.org; www.nicolasberggruenfoundation.com; en.wikipedia.org; berggruen.org; www.berggruen.org; berggruen.org
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Berggruen Institute, funded by the Trust, focuses on 'renovating democracy' and developing governance frameworks for the age of globalization and digital capitalism. Its research addresses AI governance through a philosophical and institutional lens, suggesting a measured, institution-building approach rather than either acceleration or heavy restriction. The Institute's work on planetary governance and technology implies support for thoughtful regulatory frameworks rather than laissez-faire or prohibitive stances.
entities.belief_threat_models: Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Economic inequality, Loss of control

entities.name: Noemi Dreksler
entities.title: Senior Research Fellow at the Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI); Survey Researcher & Psychological Scientist specializing in AI governance and risk management
entities.primary_org: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)
entities.notes: Noemi Dreksler is a Senior Research Fellow at the Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI), where she leads the organization's survey research program. She holds a DPhil in Experimental Psychology from the University of Oxford (completed 2020), supervised at the Crossmodal Research Laboratory. Her survey work has spanned multiple influential studies including a large 2019 survey of ML researchers on AI progress and ethics (N=524), a two-wave survey of 500+ local US elected officials in 2022 and 2023 capturing pre- and post-ChatGPT attitudes, a survey of 51 leading AGI safety and governance experts, and public opinion surveys across 11 countries with over 13,000 respondents. She also led development of the AI Survey Hub for Attitudes and Research Exchange (AI SHARE) database aggregating 200+ studies from 2014–2023. She received $231,608 from the AI Risk Mitigation Fund for a two-year project conducting public and expert surveys on AI governance and forecasting. More recently her focus has pivoted to AI risk management practices, particularly frontier AI companies' safety frameworks, including co-authoring work on marginal risk assessment methodologies.
entities.notes_sources: www.governance.ai; cdn.governance.ai; noemidreksler.com; noemidreksler.com; www.governance.ai; www.noemidreksler.com; governance.ai; www.governance.ai
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Dreksler's research explicitly advocates for pre-deployment risk assessments, dangerous capabilities evaluations, third-party model audits, safety restrictions on model usage, and red teaming for frontier/AGI-level systems. Her survey of 51 experts found 'overwhelming support' for these governance practices. Her work on open-sourcing highly capable foundation models argues that for some highly capable models, risks of open-sourcing may outweigh benefits, suggesting support for targeted restrictions on frontier AI.
entities.belief_threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Bias/discrimination, Misinformation, National security, Weapons proliferation

entities.name: North Carolina General Assembly
entities.notes: The North Carolina General Assembly is the bicameral state legislature of North Carolina, comprising the Senate and House of Representatives. It shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through large-scale funding of research and workforce programs within the UNC System. In 2023, the General Assembly passed a landmark biennial budget (S.L. 2023-134) that included a $500 million endowment to NCInnovation, a nonprofit university research accelerator tasked with funding applied research—including AI-enabled tools—across UNC System institutions. The Assembly appropriated $29 million (distributed July 2024) to expand nursing education at 12 public universities, partly leveraging advanced simulation and technology-based training. The legislature also appropriated over $67 million in new funds to the North Carolina Collaboratory for research in service to the state, including oversight of $140 million for NC Innovation project funds during the 2023–25 biennium. NCInnovation must report annual outcome data to the General Assembly, including grants awarded and jobs created, and the legislature retains the power to dissolve the organization and reclaim assets. As of late 2025, both House and Senate budget versions contained provisions to claw back the $500 million NCInnovation endowment, reflecting ongoing legislative oversight of major research investments.
entities.notes_sources: www.northcarolina.edu; nsjonline.com; collaboratory.unc.edu; inside.charlotte.edu; www.northcarolina.edu; webservices.ncleg.gov; www.northcarolina.edu; www.heraldsun.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The General Assembly has shown a strong pro-investment posture toward emerging technology research (e.g., $500M to NCInnovation, large UNC System research appropriations) suggesting an accelerationist tendency toward AI-related R&D. However, no explicit AI regulatory stance or legislation is evidenced in the sources; the legislature exercises oversight through funding conditions and reporting requirements rather than direct AI regulation.

entities.name: NYU Center for Mind, Ethics, and Policy (CMEP)
entities.notes: The NYU Center for Mind, Ethics, and Policy (CMEP) is an endowed academic research center at New York University's College of Arts & Science, formally launched in Fall 2024 after transitioning from the Mind, Ethics, and Policy Program (MEP), which was founded in 2022. The center is directed by Jeff Sebo, Associate Professor of Environmental Studies at NYU, who also directs the Center for Environmental and Animal Protection (CEAP) and co-directs the Wild Animal Welfare Program. CMEP's mission is to advance understanding of the consciousness, sentience, sapience, moral status, legal status, and political status of nonhuman beings—both biological and artificial—through research, teaching, outreach, and field building across science, philosophy, and policy. The $6 million endowment established in May 2024 comprises a $5 million gift from The Navigation Fund and $1 million from Polaris Ventures (also referred to as Macroscopic Ventures in some sources). CMEP is described as the first center to integrate science, ethics, and policy research on animal and AI minds together, with philosopher Kristin Andrews calling it 'a game changer for research in animal mind and cognition.' The center directly shapes the AI policy ecosystem by researching the moral and legal status of AI systems and advocating for policy frameworks that account for AI sentience and moral significance. It is affiliated with the effective altruism and longtermist philanthropic community through its funders.
entities.notes_sources: as.nyu.edu; beta.effectivealtruism.org; www.nyu.edu; forum.effectivealtruism.org; sites.google.com; dailynous.com; nyunews.com; nyunews.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: CMEP advocates for policy frameworks that recognize the moral and legal status of AI systems and other nonhumans, implying a targeted regulatory approach requiring legal and political protections for AI entities. The center engages in field building across science, philosophy, and policy, suggesting support for evidence-based, measured governance rather than either deregulation or blanket restrictions.
entities.belief_threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Power concentration, Loss of control

entities.name: Okawa Foundation for Information and Telecommunications
entities.notes: The Okawa Foundation for Information and Telecommunications is a Japanese public foundation established by information technology pioneer Isao Okawa, with a mission to promote and develop the field of information and communications technology. It operates two major programs: the Okawa Prize (a prestigious award with a ¥10 million cash component recognizing outstanding contributions to research, technological development, and business in information and telecommunications) and the Okawa Research Grant, awarded annually to early-career researchers in the United States. The Research Grant has supported numerous AI-relevant researchers at top U.S. universities, including Anca Dragan (robotics/AI safety at UC Berkeley), Chelsea Finn (2023, generality and dexterity in visuomotor control at Stanford), Dawn Song (2008, computer security at UC Berkeley), Amanda Coston (contextual evaluation of AI at UC Berkeley), Azalia Mirhoseini (self-improving AI systems at Stanford), and several others working on LLMs, diffusion models, and AI reasoning. The foundation has been active for over 35 years and consistently funds assistant professors at leading U.S. research universities working on cutting-edge AI and computing topics. Its selection committee includes prominent academics from Waseda University, Osaka University, Carnegie Mellon, and other institutions. Through its grant and prize programs, the foundation meaningfully shapes the U.S. AI research ecosystem by providing early-career funding to researchers working on AI safety, machine learning, and AI systems.
entities.notes_sources: www.eecs.berkeley.edu; en.wikipedia.org; shwetak.com; www.okawa-foundation.or.jp; www.okawa-foundation.or.jp; homes.cs.washington.edu; ee.stanford.edu; samueli.ucla.edu
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The foundation does not appear to take explicit public positions on AI regulation. Its activities are focused on research promotion and awards rather than policy advocacy, making its regulatory stance unclear from available sources.

entities.name: Okta, Inc.
entities.notes: Okta, Inc. (NASDAQ: OKTA) is the world's leading independent identity management platform, providing authentication, authorization, single sign-on (SSO), and multi-factor authentication (MFA) solutions to enterprises globally. The company describes itself as 'The World's Identity Company' and explicitly positions its technology as securing AI, machine, and human identities. Okta Ventures, the company's investment arm, invests in companies focused on identity, security, and privacy, including workload IAM provider Aembit (2023) and verifiable credentials provider Accredify (2024), directly shaping the AI and cybersecurity ecosystem. Through its philanthropic arm, Okta for Good, the company has committed over $10 million through a donor-advised fund held at Tides Foundation, including a $100,000 grant to UC Berkeley's Center for Long-Term Cybersecurity (CLTC) in June 2023 to support the Cybersecurity Futures 2030 project identifying emerging cybersecurity trends. Okta launched a $1M+ Nonprofit Cybersecurity Portfolio in June 2022 to improve security across the social sector, and in October 2023 announced $1.6 million in philanthropic grants for cybersecurity workforce development targeting underrepresented communities. The company acquired Auth0 in May 2021, expanding its identity platform significantly. Okta's identity infrastructure is foundational to AI deployments, as organizations securing AI agents, users, and workflows depend on its platform.
entities.notes_sources: okta.com; okta.com; okta.com; www.okta.com; www.okta.com; www.prnewswire.com; www.okta.com; www.okta.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Okta positions itself as a proactive partner in cybersecurity governance, funding cross-sector dialogue on emerging cybersecurity risks via CLTC's Cybersecurity Futures 2030 project and supporting nonprofit and civil society cybersecurity resilience. Their philanthropic investments suggest support for collaborative, multi-stakeholder approaches to security governance rather than purely market-driven solutions, though no explicit public stance on AI-specific legislation is evident from sources.

entities.name: Oregon State University
entities.notes: Oregon State University (OSU) is a major public research university and one of only three institutions in the U.S. designated as a land, sea, space, and sun grant institution, with campuses in Corvallis and Bend, Oregon. OSU is building the Jen-Hsun and Lori Mills Huang Collaborative Innovation Complex (CIC), a $213 million, 143,000-square-foot advanced research and education facility focused on artificial intelligence, materials science, robotics, and semiconductor research, with a groundbreaking ceremony held April 12, 2024. The CIC was catalyzed by a $50 million gift from NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang and his wife Lori (both OSU alumni) announced October 14, 2022, matched by another $50 million from the Wayne and Gladys Valley Foundation, plus $72 million in Oregon state capital bonds. The facility will house one of the nation's most powerful university supercomputers and is expected to open in 2025. OSU's strategic plan targets nearly doubling research expenditures to $600 million (from $367 million in FY2023), with the CIC serving as a centerpiece for AI-driven discovery and industry partnerships, including Oregon's semiconductor sector. The university is also running its 'Believe It: The Campaign for Oregon State University' fundraising campaign seeking $1.75 billion, with over $1 billion already committed as of the campaign's launch.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: OSU has not made explicit public statements on AI regulation as an institution. Its actions — building a major AI and robotics research complex, partnering with industry (semiconductor, tech), and seeking state and philanthropic funding — suggest a pro-development orientation focused on research and workforce training rather than advocacy for or against regulatory frameworks.
entities.belief_threat_models: National security, Labor displacement, Economic inequality

entities.name: Overland AI
entities.notes: Overland AI is a defense technology company founded in 2022 and headquartered in Seattle, Washington, specializing in advanced autonomy for uncrewed ground vehicles (UGVs) for U.S. defense and national security applications. The company was founded by CEO Byron Boots, formerly of the University of Washington's Robot Learning Lab, and co-founder and President Stephanie Bonk. In January 2025, Overland raised a $32 million Series A round led by 8VC, with participation from Point72 Ventures, Overmatch Ventures, Shasta Ventures, Ascend, Osage University Partners, and Caprock. In February 2026, the company raised an additional $100 million equity round led by 8VC, including a $20 million venture debt facility from TriplePoint Capital, to scale operations with U.S. Armed Forces. Overland's flagship software product, OverWatch, enables a single operator to coordinate a fleet of autonomous ground vehicles in complex, contested, off-road, and GPS-denied environments. The company partnered with L3Harris Technologies on the Diamondback autonomous ground vehicle, unveiled at the Association of the U.S. Army conference in October 2024. Overland AI shapes the AI ecosystem by developing and deploying AI-driven autonomy software and hardware stacks for military ground systems, competing with players such as Applied Intuition Defense, Forterra, Kodiak Robotics, and Neya Systems.
entities.notes_sources: www.overland.ai; www.axios.com; www.8vc.com; www.overland.ai; www.overland.ai; www.globenewswire.com; startup-weekly.com; www.overland.ai
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Overland AI's public statements emphasize accelerating deployment of autonomous ground systems to give the U.S. a 'decisive advantage' and 'terrify adversaries,' with no mention of regulatory constraints. Their investor 8VC, which guides their strategic positioning, is associated with a pro-innovation, minimal-regulation stance in defense tech.

entities.name: Owl Ventures
entities.notes: Owl Ventures is the world's largest venture capital firm focused exclusively on education technology (EdTech), founded in 2014 and headquartered in San Francisco with additional offices in Boston and London. As of early 2026, the firm manages $2.2B+ AUM across seven funds, having started with a $100M fund in 2015, grown to $1.2B+ AUM by 2021, and surpassed $2B after closing over $1 billion in new funds in January 2022 (including a $640M fifth fund and $270M Opportunity Fund II). The firm invests across all stages from pre-seed through IPO, with initial check sizes typically in the $5M–$50M range, and has backed notable EdTech unicorns including BYJU's, Degreed, MasterClass, Quizlet, Apna, Newsela, Greenlight, and Stash. Owl Ventures shapes the AI ecosystem specifically through its focus on AI-driven learning and workforce development, including investments in Workera (an AI-powered skills assessment platform co-founded by Andrew Ng's AI Fund), Sizzle AI, and Cloudforce (which secured $10M Series A from Owl Ventures and Microsoft for safe, equitable AI in education and healthcare). The firm's investment thesis emphasizes measurable educational outcomes, equity, and global access, with coverage spanning six continents and LP networks including foundations, university endowments, family offices, and sovereign wealth funds. Managing Directors include Ian Chiu, Tom Costin, Amit Patel, and Tory Patterson.
entities.notes_sources: www.owlvc.com; www.owlvc.com; www.bloomberg.com; www.owlvc.com; www.owlvc.com; www.owlvc.com; pe-insights.com; www.gaebler.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit policy statements found in sources, but Owl Ventures' investment focus on AI-driven learning, its backing of companies building 'safe, equitable AI' (e.g., Cloudforce), and its mission-driven framing suggest a preference for industry-led responsible development over heavy regulatory mandates. Their emphasis on outcomes and equity implies acceptance of some guardrails but primarily through market and mission mechanisms.

entities.name: Pegatron Corporation
entities.notes: Pegatron Corporation (stylized PEGΛTRON) is a Taiwanese electronics manufacturer founded January 1, 2008, spun off from Asus on June 1, 2010, specializing in design and manufacturing services (DMS) for computing, communications, and consumer electronics. With revenue of approximately US$40.4 billion (2023) and operations in Taiwan, China, Czech Republic, Mexico, Indonesia, and the US, Pegatron is one of the world's largest contract electronics manufacturers. In October 2025, Pegatron and MIT CSAIL announced a landmark five-year research partnership (2026–2031) focused on developing physically and emotionally intelligent robotic systems, led by CSAIL Director Daniela Rus and Pegatron's Alan Lin. The collaboration spans robotic manipulation, tactile sensing, multimodal perception, adaptive hands, flexible electronic skins, and AI-driven control frameworks, with the goal of deploying robots in precision assembly, collaborative manufacturing, logistics, and assistive robotics. Pegatron's strategic rationale is to gain early access to cutting-edge robotics research and translate it into industrial deployable solutions at scale. The partnership also aims to produce open-source software frameworks and prototype hardware, positioning Pegatron at the intersection of AI research and large-scale manufacturing. Pegatron is also listed as a member of MIT CSAIL's industry affiliates program through the Epoch Foundation's long-standing MIT collaboration network.

Their actions — investing in a five-year robotics AI research partnership with MIT CSAIL focused on safe, human-collaborative robots — suggest an implicit preference for responsible but accelerated AI development in industrial contexts, but no formal regulatory position has been articulated.

entities.name: Phil Black
entities.title: Co-Founder and General Partner at True Ventures; early-stage venture capitalist and angel investor
entities.other_orgs: Blacksmith Capital (founder), Mythos Ventures (individual investor), StrongDM (board member), Inventables (board member)
entities.notes: Phil Black is co-founder and General Partner of True Ventures, a Silicon Valley-based early-stage venture capital firm he co-founded in 2005 alongside Jon Callaghan. True Ventures has committed over $3.8 billion across multiple funds, backing more than 1,050 founders across 500+ companies and creating over 85,000 jobs worldwide. The firm invests at seed and Series A stages in technology sectors including AI, robotics, SaaS, consumer tech, climate technologies, and therapeutics, with notable portfolio companies including Fitbit, Peloton, Ring, and Automattic (WordPress). Prior to True Ventures, Black founded Blacksmith Capital, a small angel fund that served as a formative moment in his venture career, and he also held general partner positions for eight years at other firms including Summit Partners. He shapes the AI ecosystem directly through True Ventures investments in AI companies such as Anthropic and Answer.AI, and as an individual investor in Mythos Ventures ($14M round). Black also serves on the boards of StrongDM and Inventables. His investment philosophy emphasizes founder-centric support, risk-taking at the earliest stage, and backing bold, product-driven founders.

entities.name: Playground Global
entities.notes: Playground Global is an early-stage venture capital firm investing in deep technology companies at the intersection of science and engineering. The firm focuses on pioneering solutions in artificial intelligence, automation, infrastructure, life sciences, decarbonization, and next-generation compute. Playground Global co-led Agility Robotics' $20 million funding round in October 2020 alongside DCVC, joined by TDK Ventures, MFV Partners, ITIC, Sony Innovation Fund, and Safar Partners. The firm then co-led Agility Robotics' $150 million Series B round in April 2022, again alongside DCVC, with the Amazon Industrial Innovation Fund also participating. Bruce Leak, a General Partner at Playground Global, serves on Agility Robotics' board of directors and has been involved since the company's earliest days. Playground's investment thesis emphasizes complex, difficult technical challenges and leverages deep technical and operational expertise to help founders achieve breakthroughs needed to build transformational businesses. Their consistent co-investment with DCVC in robotics and AI-adjacent technologies signals a strong focus on automation and physical AI applications.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance is stated in available sources. However, Playground's investment strategy emphasizing rapid deep-tech innovation, automation, and scaling transformational businesses suggests a preference for light-touch regulation that allows frontier technology companies to develop and deploy quickly. This is inferred from their investment actions rather than explicit public statements.

entities.name: Plural Platform
entities.notes: Plural Platform is a European early-stage venture capital firm launched in June 2022 by a group of prominent tech founders including Ian Hogarth (Songkick co-founder), Taavet Hinrikus (Wise co-founder), Sten Tamkivi (Teleport co-founder), and Khaled Heloui (former Bigpoint CEO). The fund launched with a €250 million vehicle (Plural Platform SCSp RAIF) focused on backing early-stage European tech companies with global ambitions, making investments typically between €1 million and €10 million per deal. Its model is distinctive in that all investors are exclusively former founders and operators, emphasizing 'hands-on' support drawing on direct company-building experience. Plural is relevant to the AI policy ecosystem through its investment in AI safety and alignment companies, most notably its lead role in Conjecture's $5 million seed round in November 2022 — Conjecture being a prominent AI safety research organization. Its investment areas include Artificial Intelligence alongside fintech, digital media, and e-commerce. The fund's portfolio also includes companies like Isometric, Metaview, and CoMind, reflecting a broad technology focus with AI as a key vertical. Ian Hogarth, one of Plural's co-founders, is a well-known AI governance voice in the UK and later chaired the UK AI Safety Institute, lending the fund indirect influence in AI policy circles.
entities.notes_sources: founderlodge.com; www.eu-startups.com; pitchbook.com; www.uktech.news; www.eu-startups.com; pluralplatform.com; o.parsers.vc; via.tt.se
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Plural's investment in Conjecture, an AI safety-focused organization, signals alignment with responsible AI development. Co-founder Ian Hogarth has publicly written about AI risk and later chaired the UK AI Safety Institute, suggesting the fund's leadership leans toward measured, safety-conscious governance rather than pure acceleration or heavy restriction.
entities.belief_threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

entities.name: Prime Intellect
entities.notes: Prime Intellect is a decentralized AI infrastructure and research company founded in 2023 by Vincent Weisser and Johannes Hagemann, focused on building the 'Open Superintelligence Stack' for globally distributed AI training. The company raised $5.5M in seed funding in April 2024 co-led by CoinFund and Distributed Global, followed by a $15M Series A led by Founders Fund with participation from Andrej Karpathy, Clem Delangue (Hugging Face), Tri Dao (Together.AI), and others, bringing total funding to over $20M. Prime Intellect developed PRIME, a fault-tolerant distributed training framework using DiLoCo algorithms that reduces communication bandwidth by 400x compared to traditional data-parallel training. Their landmark achievements include INTELLECT-1 (the first 10B parameter model trained across globally distributed infrastructure, released late 2024), INTELLECT-2 (the first 32B parameter model trained via globally distributed reinforcement learning, May 2025), and INTELLECT-3 (a 100B+ parameter Mixture-of-Experts model, November 2025). The company provides compute grants to independent AI researchers—as evidenced by their support of researcher Theia Vogel—and aggregates GPU supply from centralized and decentralized providers globally. Prime Intellect open-sources its models, checkpoints, training frameworks, and datasets, explicitly aiming to prevent consolidation of AI capabilities within a few large organizations.
entities.notes_sources: arxiv.org; www.theblock.co; www.primeintellect.ai; www.primeintellect.ai; www.primeintellect.ai; www.cbinsights.com; primeintellect.ai; www.primeintellect.ai
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Prime Intellect's mission explicitly frames decentralized, open-source AI development as a counter to centralized control, stating their work 'prevents the consolidation of AI capabilities within a few organizations.' Their platform invites anyone globally to contribute compute and participate in AI training, and their fundraise announcement focuses on democratizing access rather than governance frameworks. No regulatory advocacy positions are stated; their stance is inferred from strong pro-acceleration, anti-centralization rhetoric and open-source-first strategy.

entities.name: Principles of Intelligence
entities.notes: Principles of Intelligence (PrincInt) is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit research organization incorporated in Minnesota (EIN 33-4129337) focused on building scientific foundations for AI safety through interdisciplinary research. It evolved from PIBBSS (Principles of Intelligent Behavior in Biological and Social Systems), co-founded in late 2021 by Nora Ammann, Tushita (TJ) Jha, and Anna Gajdova, and rebranded as Principles of Intelligence as the umbrella organization expanded. Its flagship program is the PIBBSS Summer Research Fellowship, which recruits researchers from ecology, neuroscience, economics, physics, and other disciplines to work on AI safety problems; since 2025 it has added a dedicated cooperative AI track funded by the Cooperative AI Foundation. Beyond the fellowship, PrincInt runs an Affiliateship program (6-12 months of funding for researchers to develop early-stage ideas into prototypes that can spin off into new organizations) and a Horizon Scanning initiative that proactively identifies novel research directions and senior academic talent for AI safety. The organization is funded by Open Philanthropy, Survival and Flourishing Fund, AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund, and the Cooperative AI Foundation. Alumni have gone on to found organizations (e.g., Simplex), join Anthropic and Epoch AI, and work at the UK AI Security Institute. PrincInt aspires to become a 'Bell Labs of AI Safety,' hosting multiple interdisciplinary research threads and catalyzing a 'Big Science of AI Safety.'
entities.notes_sources: princint.org; princint.ai; princint.ai; princint.ai; princint.ai; princint.ai; princint.ai; princint.ai
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: PrincInt does not explicitly publish policy positions, but research outputs include work on AI tort liability reform (proposing strict liability, mandatory insurance, and pre-announced liability rules for AI developers) and analysis of AI safety as an emerging scientific paradigm. Their orientation is toward building rigorous scientific and institutional foundations for safe AI rather than advocating for specific regulatory frameworks, suggesting a moderate stance focused on grounding governance in sound science.
entities.belief_threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Economic inequality, Labor displacement

entities.name: Public Interest Tech Lab (Harvard Kennedy School)
entities.notes: The Public Interest Tech Lab (PITL) is a research initiative launched in June 2021 at Harvard Kennedy School's Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy, founded and directed by Dr. Latanya Sweeney, the Daniel Paul Professor of the Practice of Government and Technology at Harvard. It was established with a $3 million grant from the Ford Foundation. The lab's mission is to ensure technology products are socially responsible, focusing on ways technology can serve—rather than harm—people and society, with particular emphasis on data privacy, algorithmic fairness, and equity. It operates across three pillars: creating and providing public-interest technologies, enabling research and development, and sharing knowledge across institutions and disciplines via networks like the Public Interest Technology University Network. The lab grew out of Sweeney's earlier Data Privacy Lab, founded at Carnegie Mellon in 2001 and brought to Harvard in 2011, reflecting decades of work on technology-society clashes. Sweeney, a former Chief Technology Officer at the U.S. Federal Trade Commission and the first Black woman to earn a PhD in Computer Science from MIT, brings a strong regulatory and policy perspective to the lab's work. Research areas include voting, vaccine access, algorithmic discrimination, and other societal challenges with technological implications, shaping both academic discourse and policy on AI and data systems.
entities.notes_sources: techlab.org; shorensteincenter.org; blog.epic.org; news.harvard.edu; techlab.org; shorensteincenter.org; researchmethodscommunity.sagepub.com; nationaltechnology.co.uk
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The lab's core mission is to ensure technology products are 'socially responsible,' framing harms from algorithms and data systems as unresolved societal clashes requiring active intervention. Sweeney's prior role as FTC CTO and her research on algorithmic discrimination and data privacy signal support for robust oversight and accountability requirements, not voluntary self-governance.
entities.belief_threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

entities.name: Quantitative Foundation
entities.notes: The Quantitative Foundation is a Charlottesville-based private philanthropic foundation founded and led by Jaffray Woodriff, a 1991 UVA (McIntire) graduate and pioneer of data-science-driven quantitative investing. The foundation's most notable act was a landmark $120 million grant to the University of Virginia in 2019 to establish UVA's School of Data Science — the largest individual private grant in UVA's history and one that created the first standalone school of data science in the United States. In December 2024, the foundation committed an additional $20 million to UVA to fund construction of a new building adjacent to the School of Data Science, intended to support expanded research, teaching, and entrepreneurial initiatives including the UVA Innovates program. The foundation shapes the AI ecosystem by directly funding the institutional infrastructure for data science and AI education, research, and workforce development. The 2019 gift also supports faculty endowments, student scholarships, and visiting fellows at the School of Data Science. William Foshay serves as executive director of the foundation and represents it publicly. Through its investments in data science education and AI-focused research capacity, the Quantitative Foundation is a significant philanthropic force in the academic AI pipeline.
entities.notes_sources: datascience.virginia.edu; www.cavalierdaily.com; news.virginia.edu; www.eurekalert.org; philanthropynewsdigest.org; cvillerightnow.com; giving.virginia.edu; pebourne.wordpress.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit statements on AI regulation are found in available sources. The foundation's funding priorities emphasize expanding AI/data science capabilities, entrepreneurship, and public-private partnerships, suggesting a broadly pro-development orientation, but no formal regulatory stance has been articulated.

entities.name: Qube Research & Technologies
entities.notes: Qube Research & Technologies (QRT) is a quantitative trading and technology firm that applies advanced mathematics, statistics, computer science, and engineering to financial markets. Co-founded by Laurent Laizet (Chief Investment Officer), QRT has made a major philanthropic commitment to academic research through the launch of QRT Labs in 2026, a coordinated multi-university initiative spanning Imperial College London, the University of Cambridge, and the University of Oxford. Through QRT Labs, the firm directly funds more than 70 early-career researchers including PhD/DPhil students and postdoctoral researchers across the three institutions. Research priorities include foundation AI models, agentic systems, high-performance computing, cybersecurity, hardware design, advanced mathematical modelling, and complex systems — areas directly relevant to the future of AI. The initiative is structured to preserve academic independence while fostering sustained collaboration between QRT technologists and academic researchers. QRT's philanthropic investment positions it as a significant funder of AI-relevant fundamental research in the UK academic ecosystem, with potential long-term influence on the pipeline of AI researchers and the direction of foundational AI science.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: QRT's public statements emphasize 'intellectual freedom,' 'academic independence,' and long-term trust-based collaboration rather than regulatory frameworks. Their investment model focuses on enabling free scientific inquiry without reference to regulatory oversight of AI, suggesting a preference for self-governance and open research.

entities.name: Rad AI
entities.notes: Rad AI is a San Francisco-based healthcare AI company founded in 2018 by Jeff Chang, M.D., described as the youngest US radiologist in history, and co-founded with CEO Doktor Gurson. The company pioneered one of the first commercial generative AI products in healthcare, building AI tools that automatically generate radiology reports customized to each radiologist's language and style. Its flagship products include Rad AI Reporting (AI-enabled radiology reporting workflow) and Rad AI Continuity (patient follow-up management), which collectively impact nearly 50 million patients annually. In May 2024, Rad AI closed a $50 million Series B round led by Khosla Ventures, with participation from WiL, ARTIS Ventures, OCV Partners, Kickstart Fund, and Gradient Ventures (Google's AI fund), bringing total funding to over $80 million. The company is used by more than one-third of all US health systems and 9 of the 10 largest US radiology practices, with revenue tripling year-over-year. Rad AI has partnered with Google to use MedLM and cloud infrastructure, and has trained proprietary LLMs on some of the world's largest radiology datasets. The company has been recognized by CB Insights (Digital Health 50, AI 100) and AuntMinnie (Best New Radiology Software 2023, Best New Radiology Vendor 2021), and an Accenture report named it the highest-influence private company in radiology as of December 2023.
entities.notes_sources: www.radai.com; www.fiercehealthcare.com; precursorvc.com; www.healthcareittoday.com; hlth.com; www.ocvpartners.com; hitconsultant.net; www.prnewswire.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory policy positions are stated in available sources. The company's focus on reducing errors, improving patient outcomes, and partnering with major health systems suggests comfort operating within existing healthcare regulatory frameworks, but no formal stance on AI regulation has been publicly articulated.

entities.name: Recursion Pharmaceuticals
entities.notes: Recursion Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ: RXRX) is a clinical-stage TechBio company founded in 2013 by Chris Gibson, Blake Borgeson, and Dean Li at the University of Utah, focused on using AI and automation to industrialize drug discovery and development. The company operates the Recursion OS, a closed-loop platform combining high-throughput wet-lab biology with machine learning to generate one of the world's largest proprietary biological and chemical datasets (50+ petabytes as of 2025). Recursion completed its IPO on NASDAQ in 2021 and has raised funding across multiple rounds, with Lux Capital leading its Series A in 2017 and a $60M Series B shortly after. The company has built BioHive-2, ranked as the 76th most powerful supercomputer globally (Top500, 2025), developed in partnership with NVIDIA, and has expanded partnerships with Google Cloud and Tempus. In 2023-2024, Recursion acquired Cyclica and Valence to bolster generative AI and digital chemistry capabilities, and announced a combination with Exscientia to advance AI-driven drug industrialization. The company is a prominent example of AI applied to pharmaceutical R&D at scale, with a pipeline of 50+ programs across oncology and rare diseases, and serves as a key builder and deployer of AI in biotech.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Recursion's public messaging emphasizes speed, disruption of the status quo, and removing human bias through AI-driven empiricism. The company frames itself as challenging conservative norms in pharma and advocates for industrializing drug discovery through automation and AI with minimal friction. No explicit support for AI regulation has been identified in available sources; their philosophy centers on accelerating innovation.

entities.name: Renaissance Philanthropy
entities.notes: Renaissance Philanthropy is a philanthropic advisory organization founded in May 2024 by Tom Kalil (CEO) and Kumar Garg (President), both veterans of the Obama-era White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) and Schmidt Futures. The organization was established with founding donations from Eric and Wendy Schmidt, though the exact amount has not been disclosed. Its core mission is to fuel a '21st-century renaissance' by connecting high-net-worth individuals, families, and foundations with transformative science, technology, and innovation projects that fall through the gaps between venture capital and government funding. Renaissance builds time-bound, thesis-driven philanthropic funds, allowing donors to support breakthrough ideas without needing large in-house expert teams. AI is explicitly within its scope, as the organization is affiliated with Schmidt Futures initiatives such as Future House, a 'moonshot' aimed at creating an 'AI Scientist.' Kalil previously played key roles in the $40 billion National Nanotechnology Initiative and the BRAIN Initiative under Clinton and Obama. In its first year (2025 Annual Report), Renaissance reported progress moving from 'proof-of-concept to proof-of-impact,' with funding partners including philanthropists, foundations, and government agencies.
entities.notes_sources: www.philanthropy.com; www.renaissancephilanthropy.org; www.insidephilanthropy.com; www.renaissancephilanthropy.org; renaissancephilanthropy.org; www.renaissancephilanthropy.org; renphil.org; www.insidephilanthropy.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Renaissance Philanthropy's mission centers on accelerating science and technology innovation through private philanthropy, explicitly framing declining public R&D investment as an opportunity for philanthropy to step up. Its focus on funding projects that venture capital and government agencies pass over suggests a preference for philanthropic and market-driven solutions over regulatory intervention. No explicit regulatory policy positions are stated.

entities.name: Richard King Mellon Foundation
entities.notes: The Richard King Mellon Foundation is a major Pittsburgh-based philanthropic foundation with a 74+ year history of supporting science, technology, and regional development in western Pennsylvania. In May 2021, the Foundation approved a $150 million grant to Carnegie Mellon University — the largest single grant in its history — to accelerate CMU's science and technology leadership. The grant was split into two $75 million tranches: one as a lead gift for a new cutting-edge science building on CMU's Oakland campus, and the other to seed the Robotics Innovation Center (RIC) and the Manufacturing Futures Institute at Hazelwood Green, a former riverside steel mill site. Within the $75 million robotics/manufacturing allocation, $45 million serves as the lead grant for the RIC, a planned 150,000 square-foot translational robotics research facility estimated to cost over $100 million total. The RIC is designed to support robotics research, integration, iteration, and commercialization, including pre-incubator space for CMU-affiliated robotics startups. Through these investments, the Foundation is a significant indirect shaper of the AI and robotics research ecosystem, funding infrastructure that supports one of the world's leading robotics and AI universities. The Foundation's focus on Pittsburgh's economic transformation through advanced manufacturing, robotics, and science aligns its philanthropic strategy closely with the future of AI and automation.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit statements on AI regulation are present in the available sources. The Foundation's actions suggest a pro-development, acceleration-oriented stance through large investments in robotics and AI research infrastructure at CMU, but no formal regulatory position has been articulated.

entities.name: RTX Ventures
entities.notes: RTX Ventures is the venture capital arm of RTX (formerly Raytheon Technologies, NYSE: RTX), one of the largest aerospace and defense contractors in the world. Led by President and Managing Director Dan (Daniel) Ateya, RTX Ventures invests in early-stage companies focused on transforming aerospace and defense, with portfolio areas including hypersonics, artificial intelligence, machine learning, advanced manufacturing, sustainable aviation, and space. The fund made its first eight investments in 2022, backing companies such as H55 (electric propulsion, October 2022) and Hermeus (hypersonics). In July 2023, RTX Ventures led a $45 million Series A round for Impulse Space, an in-space transportation company. In June 2025, RTX Ventures co-led a $5.5 million seed round for Phoenix Semiconductor, focused on domestically manufactured legacy chips for national security applications. In July 2025, RTX Ventures led a $55 million Series Seed+ round for Radical AI, a NYC-based company building autonomous labs for materials R&D using AI, alongside NVIDIA's NVentures, noa, Eni Next, Infinite Capital, and AlleyCorp. RTX Ventures shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its role as a funder of AI-driven defense, materials science, and aerospace startups, leveraging RTX's engineering resources to accelerate portfolio company growth.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation found in sources. RTX Ventures' investment focus on defense and national security AI applications suggests comfort with government oversight frameworks, but no specific regulatory stance has been articulated publicly.

entities.name: Russell Sage Foundation
entities.notes: The Russell Sage Foundation (RSF) was established in 1907 by Margaret Olivia Sage 'for the improvement of social and living conditions in the United States' and is the principal American foundation devoted exclusively to social sciences. RSF funds social science research across four main programs: Behavioral Science and Decision Making in Context; the Future of Work; Race, Ethnicity, and Immigration; and Social, Political, and Economic Inequality. It shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its Future of Work program, which funds research on how AI and automation affect jobs, wages, and inequality — including a fellowship supporting Andrew McAfee (MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy) to write a book on AI's economic and social implications, and grants studying AI's impact on labor markets using longitudinal data. RSF also supported the Stanford Digital Economy Lab, which conducts empirical research on AI's labor market effects. RSF publishes books and the peer-reviewed, open-access journal RSF: The Russell Sage Foundation Journal of the Social Sciences, and hosts visiting scholars at its New York headquarters. Notable funded research includes Till von Wachter's study of AI's effects on employment and earnings in California over 20 years, and multiple grants examining technological change, wage transparency, and labor market restructuring. RSF's work contributes directly to national policy discussions on AI-driven economic disruption.
entities.notes_sources: www.russellsage.org; www.russellsage.org; www.russellsage.org; www.russellsage.org; www.russellsage.org; www.russellsage.org; www.russellsage.org; www.russellsage.org
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: RSF does not take explicit regulatory positions itself, but consistently funds research examining the labor market and inequality consequences of AI — implying concern that policy responses may be needed. The foundation's Future of Work program explicitly seeks research on 'the role of public policies on employment, earnings, and job quality,' suggesting a belief that targeted policy intervention is warranted, though RSF does not advocate directly.

entities.name: Ryan Kidd
entities.title: Co-Executive Director at MATS Research; Co-Founder & Board Member of London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA); Manifund Regrantor; AI Safety Field-Builder
entities.primary_org: MATS Research (ML Alignment & Theory Scholars Program)
entities.other_orgs: London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA), Manifund, Catalyze Impact, Halcyon Futures, Pivotal Research, AI Safety ANZ, Black in AI Safety and Ethics (BASE), Alignment Foundation
entities.notes: Ryan Kidd is the Co-Executive Director of MATS Research (ML Alignment & Theory Scholars Program), a nonprofit AI safety research fellowship and talent pipeline based in Berkeley and London, which he has helped scale from 15 scholars to 175+ fellows and has supported 530+ researchers producing 180+ research publications. He co-founded the London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA) in 2023, described as the largest AI safety hub in Europe. As a Manifund Regrantor, he has awarded approximately $400,000 to AI safety startups and projects, helping seed organizations including Catalyze Impact, Timaeus, and AI Safety Atlas. Kidd holds a PhD in Physics from the University of Queensland and previously conducted AI alignment research at the Stanford Existential Risks Initiative before pivoting to field-building. He advises multiple AI safety organizations including Halcyon Futures, Pivotal Research, AI Safety ANZ, and BASE. He is a prominent voice arguing that AI safety systematically undervalues founders and field-builders relative to researchers, and has published influential essays on this theme on LessWrong. MATS alumni have founded 30+ AI safety organizations and teams, including Apollo Research, Timaeus, and Workshop Labs (acquired by Thinking Machines).

He supports building governance infrastructure (safety orgs, auditing orgs, gov-funded AI safety teams) rather than explicit hard regulation, suggesting a moderate/targeted stance focused on building the ecosystem of safety-oriented institutions rather than mandating restrictions on developers.

entities.name: Sam Bankman-Fried
entities.title: Founder and former CEO of FTX Trading Ltd. (2019–2022); Co-founder of Alameda Research; Effective Altruism-aligned philanthropist; convicted of fraud and sentenced to 25 years in prison (2024)
entities.other_orgs: Alameda Research, Jane Street Capital (former), Centre for Effective Altruism (former), FTX Future Fund (defunct), 80,000 Hours (donor)
entities.notes: Samuel Benjamin Bankman-Fried (born March 6, 1992) is the founder of FTX Trading Ltd., a cryptocurrency derivatives exchange, and co-founder of Alameda Research, a quantitative crypto trading firm. He was a major funder of effective altruism (EA)-aligned organizations working on AI safety and existential risk, including 80,000 Hours and Conjecture, making him a significant financial actor in the AI safety/policy ecosystem. Introduced to effective altruism at MIT around 2012–2013 through Will MacAskill, he pursued an 'earning to give' strategy, first at Jane Street Capital (2014–2017) and then through FTX, donating tens of millions to causes including AI safety, biosecurity, and pandemic preparedness. At his peak in 2021, his net worth reached approximately $26 billion, enabling massive philanthropic influence through the FTX Future Fund, which funded AI safety and longtermist projects. FTX collapsed in November 2022 amid revelations of mismanagement and commingling of customer funds with Alameda Research; Bankman-Fried was arrested in December 2022, convicted on seven counts of fraud, conspiracy, and money laundering in November 2023, and sentenced to 25 years in prison in March 2024, with forfeiture of over $11 billion. His collapse severely disrupted EA-aligned AI safety funding pipelines and tarnished the reputations of organizations he had supported.
entities.notes_sources: www.britannica.com; www.philanthropy.com; esp.mit.edu; 80000hours.org; www.bitstamp.net; cryptonica.news; markets.businessinsider.com; iq.wiki
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Bankman-Fried engaged extensively with U.S. Congress on cryptocurrency regulation, generally advocating for frameworks that would legitimize crypto markets without heavy restrictions. His broader EA-aligned philosophy emphasized maximizing impact through market mechanisms rather than government intervention, though he did participate in regulatory discussions suggesting openness to some industry-specific oversight.
entities.belief_threat_models: Existential risk, Power concentration, National security

entities.name: Samsung
entities.notes: Samsung is a major South Korean multinational electronics and technology conglomerate that shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through research funding and its dedicated AI research programs. Samsung's DMC (Digital Media & Communications) R&D arm awarded a $2.2M research contract in 2017 for the project 'Improving Deep Learning using Latent Structure' at NYU, with Sam Bowman as co-PI. Samsung has also funded AI research by Aaron Courville (Montreal/MILA), contributing to foundational deep learning work. Samsung operates the Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology (SAIT), which runs the Samsung AI Researcher of the Year award, honoring researchers like NYU's Kyunghyun Cho (inaugural recipient) and He He (2024 recipient), demonstrating sustained engagement with the academic AI community. Samsung has hosted events such as the Samsung AI Forum, where NYU researchers presented work on neural network models and natural language understanding. Through these funding relationships and recognition programs, Samsung plays a significant role as a funder and convener in the global AI research ecosystem, particularly in NLP and deep learning.

entities.name: Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology (SAIT)
entities.notes: Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology (SAIT) is Samsung Electronics' dedicated R&D hub focused on cutting-edge future technologies, including artificial intelligence. SAIT hosted Day 1 of the annual Samsung AI Forum 2020 (November 2-3, 2020), bringing together globally renowned AI researchers including Yoshua Bengio, Yann LeCun, and Chelsea Finn. SAIT presents the 'Samsung AI Researcher of the Year' award, with Kyunghyun Cho being one of five inaugural recipients in November 2020. The institute employs Fellows such as Professor Donhee Ham of Harvard University, who conducts research on neuromorphic computing and brain-inspired AI. SAIT's president Dr. Sungwoo Hwang co-chairs the Samsung AI Forum alongside designated 'Samsung AI Professors' such as Yoshua Bengio, reflecting the institute's strategy of partnering with top academic AI researchers worldwide. SAIT's research agenda spans foundational AI, neuromorphic computing, and cutting-edge future technologies relevant to Samsung's device and semiconductor businesses.

entities.name: Samsung Austin Semiconductor, LLC
entities.notes: Samsung Austin Semiconductor, LLC is the U.S.-based subsidiary of Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd., responsible for semiconductor fabrication operations in Texas. The company has operated in Texas for approximately 30 years and is currently constructing a major semiconductor fabrication facility in Taylor, Texas, originally announced in November 2021 as a $17 billion investment—the largest foreign direct investment in Texas history. In September 2025, Governor Greg Abbott announced a $250 million Texas Semiconductor Innovation Fund (TSIF) grant to the company for their Taylor facility, part of a broader $4.73 billion new initiative to manufacture 2-nanometer leading-edge logic chips. The Taylor facility, projected to open in 2026, is expected to contribute $26.8 billion to the Central Texas economy and create tens of thousands of jobs. Samsung's total Texas investment stands at approximately $40 billion, including plans for a second chip-manufacturing fab, an advanced packaging facility, and an R&D center in Taylor. The company's production of 2nm chips is directly relevant to AI infrastructure, as these chips are designed to power next-generation technologies including 5G, artificial intelligence, and high-performance computing. Samsung Austin Semiconductor shapes the AI ecosystem primarily as a critical semiconductor manufacturer supplying the compute hardware foundational to AI development.

entities.name: Sapphire Ventures
entities.location: Menlo Park, CA (also Austin, London, San Francisco)
entities.notes: Sapphire Ventures is a global software-focused venture capital firm with more than $10 billion in AUM and offices in Austin, London, Menlo Park, and San Francisco. Founded over two decades ago, the firm focuses on Series B through late-stage enterprise technology investments across the U.S., Europe, and Israel. Since its founding, Sapphire has invested in more than 170 companies globally, resulting in more than 30 public listings and 45 acquisitions. In November 2021, the firm announced its largest fundraise to date, bringing AUM to $8.8 billion with the closing of Sapphire Ventures Fund VI. Sapphire shapes the AI ecosystem through targeted investments in AI-driven companies, including as an existing investor in EliseAI's $250 million Series E round (August 2025), a lead investor in Constructor's $25 million Series B (June 2024), and a participant in Temporal's $300 million Series D at a $5 billion valuation. Its investment strategies span Sapphire Ventures, Sapphire Partners, and Sapphire Sport, complemented by a Portfolio Growth team that provides operational support to portfolio companies. The firm's consistent backing of vertical AI, AI infrastructure, and AI-powered enterprise software positions it as a meaningful capital allocator in the AI ecosystem.
entities.notes_sources: www.citybiz.co; www.reuters.com; eliseai.com; www.thesaasnews.com; economictimes.indiatimes.com; www.prnewswire.com; www.thesaasnews.com; sapphireventures.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit policy statements found in sources. As a growth-stage enterprise technology VC actively deploying capital into AI companies across healthcare, housing, and infrastructure, Sapphire's investment behavior strongly implies a preference for a permissive regulatory environment that enables rapid AI commercialization and scaling.

entities.name: Schwartz Reisman Institute for Technology and Society
entities.notes: The Schwartz Reisman Institute for Technology and Society (SRI) is an interdisciplinary research institute at the University of Toronto, founded through a landmark $100 million gift from Gerald Schwartz and Heather Reisman (via The Gerald Schwartz & Heather Reisman Foundation) announced in 2019—the largest donation in U of T history and the largest ever gift to the Canadian innovation sector. SRI's mission is to explore and address the ethical, social, and policy implications of AI and other emerging technologies by convening scholars from STEM, social sciences, and humanities. Its community includes 175+ researchers across 65+ departments and faculties. The institute funds graduate fellowships (100+ awarded since 2020, valued at over $1M total), supports interdisciplinary research projects, and connects researchers to grants and philanthropy. Notable affiliated researchers include David Duvenaud, Richard Zemel, and former director Gillian Hadfield (all CIFAR AI Chairs), and current director David Lie, who leads a $5.6M NSERC–CSE-funded AI safety initiative. SRI co-locates with the Vector Institute at the Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus in downtown Toronto, and its 2025–2028 strategic plan focuses on safe AI systems, data integrity, and translating research into real-world policy impact.
entities.notes_sources: srinstitute.utoronto.ca; srinstitute.utoronto.ca; srinstitute.utoronto.ca; vectorinstitute.ai; sric.utoronto.ca; www.torontosri.ca; www.torontosri.ca; www.utm.utoronto.ca
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: SRI's 2025–2028 strategic plan explicitly centers on 'steering advanced AI toward safety, ethics, and the public good' with priorities including safe AI systems, data integrity, and 'real-world policy impact.' Research includes practical compliance frameworks and responsible AI governance, indicating a balanced, targeted regulatory stance rather than accelerationist or precautionary extremes.
entities.belief_threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, National security

entities.name: Schwarz Group
entities.notes: Schwarz Group is a German multinational retail conglomerate founded in 1930 by Josef Schwarz and currently owned by Dieter Schwarz, headquartered in Neckarsulm, Baden-Württemberg. It is the largest European retailer and fourth-largest retailer globally by revenue, generating €175.4 billion in FY2024 with approximately 595,000 employees across 32 countries, operating over 14,200 stores under the Lidl and Kaufland brands. The group is relevant to the AI ecosystem primarily as a major funder of AI companies: it committed $600 million in structured financing to Cohere, a leading enterprise AI company, signaling strategic ambition in AI adoption and investment. Schwarz also holds a minority stake in Aleph Alpha, a German AI startup focused on European AI sovereignty. In 2023, Schwarz established its Schwarz Digits division encompassing ~8,000 employees and offering cloud computing (STACKIT), cybersecurity (XM Cyber, acquired 2021), artificial intelligence, and digital services explicitly designed to meet high European data protection standards and promote digital sovereignty. Schwarz Digits generated €1.9 billion in revenue in FY2024 and has strategic partnerships with Google, ServiceNow, and SAP. The group's emphasis on European digital sovereignty and investment in non-US cloud infrastructure positions it as a significant actor in shaping Europe's AI and cloud landscape.
entities.notes_sources: gruppe.schwarz; en.wikipedia.org; gruppe.schwarz; gruppe.schwarz; en.wikipedia.org; gruppe.schwarz; en.wikipedia.org; gruppe.schwarz
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Schwarz Group explicitly emphasizes compliance with 'high German data protection standards' and European data sovereignty principles through its Schwarz Digits division, suggesting support for targeted European regulatory frameworks like GDPR. Its investments in Aleph Alpha and Cohere, and its STACKIT cloud offering as a non-US alternative, reflect a preference for regulated, sovereignty-preserving AI rather than unrestrained development. No explicit opposition to AI regulation has been stated.

entities.name: Sentinel Bio
entities.notes: Sentinel Bio is a nonprofit philanthropic fund and grantmaking organization launched out of Ergo Impact as an independent 501(c)(3), founded by Claire Qureshi (CEO) and Joshua Monrad (Program Director), who previously led biosecurity grantmaking at Effective Giving. Its mission is to research and fund strategic solutions to safeguard powerful biotechnologies against misuse and prevent catastrophic pandemics. In 2025, Sentinel Bio made 36 grants totaling $7.2M, allocating $2.7M to AI-biosecurity efforts, including a $600,000 grant to SecureBio to expand AI×Bio risk evaluation into Biological Design Tools, an $85,000 grant to Epoch AI to track proliferation of bio-relevant frontier AI models and biosecurity safeguard adoption, and a $400,000 grant to the Centre for Long-Term Resilience for AI-enabled biology risk analysis. Their funded research found that only 3% of over 300 biological design tool models had any safeguards as of early 2025, and that 61.5% of the highest-risk frontier models are open source. Sentinel Bio shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through strategic philanthropic funding, evidence generation, and policy translation focused on ensuring frontier AI models are released with governance mechanisms restricting capabilities to create catastrophically harmful biological agents. They plan to raise and deploy $75M+ over the next 3-5 years, starting with $15-20M in 2026, to secure high-risk biological AI models, agents, and data.
entities.notes_sources: sentinelbio.org; sentinelbio.org; securebio.org; sentinelbio.org; securebio.substack.com; sentinelbio.org; securebio.org; gcbrupdates.substack.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Sentinel Bio explicitly supports targeted governance mechanisms for frontier AI models, specifically those with biological design capabilities. Their stated win conditions include 'securing closed-source biological AI models, protecting high-risk biological datasets, and safeguarding AI agent interactions with biological AI models.' They fund policy translation work to bridge technical findings to real-world safety improvements and engage with policymakers in the US, EU, and UK.
entities.belief_threat_models: Weapons proliferation, National security, Existential risk

entities.name: Shamil Chandaria
entities.title: Senior Research Fellow, Centre for Eudaimonia and Human Flourishing, University of Oxford; Strategic Advisor at Google DeepMind; OBE; Philanthropist and Serial Entrepreneur
entities.primary_org: University of Oxford (Centre for Eudaimonia and Human Flourishing)
entities.other_orgs: Google DeepMind, Imperial College London Centre for Psychedelic Research, Institute of Philosophy (University of London), Global Priorities Institute (funder), Harvard University (meditation neuroscience funder), UC Berkeley (meditation neuroscience funder)
entities.notes: Dr. Shamil Chandaria OBE is a multidisciplinary researcher, philanthropist, and entrepreneur with over 30 years of experience spanning computational neuroscience, machine learning, AI, and philosophy. He holds a PhD from the London School of Economics in mathematical modeling of financial systems, and an MA in Philosophy (with Distinction) from University College London. He is currently a Senior Research Fellow at the Centre for Eudaimonia and Human Flourishing at Linacre College, Oxford, and a Strategic Advisor at Google DeepMind, with prior visiting academic roles at Oxford's Future of Humanity Institute. In 2018, he helped endow the Global Priorities Institute at Oxford University, an interdisciplinary centre focused on identifying and addressing the most important issues facing humanity, including AI governance and existential risk. In 2019, he co-founded the Centre for Psychedelic Research at Imperial College London, the world's first such research center. He has also funded neuroscience of meditation research at Harvard and UC Berkeley. His AI-relevant research focuses on consciousness, the Bayesian brain, predictive processing, and the implications of these frameworks for understanding and building conscious AI systems. He was awarded an OBE in 2022 for contributions to Science, Technology, Finance, and Philanthropy.
entities.notes_sources: uk.linkedin.com; philosophy.sas.ac.uk; talks.ox.ac.uk; www.berkeleyalembic.org; www.cofo.org; www.thetripreport.com; deconstructingyourself.com; dynamic.wakingup.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit statements on AI regulation found in sources. His funding of the Global Priorities Institute (which examines existential and global priorities including AI risk) and his research on AI consciousness suggest concern about long-term AI risks, but no specific regulatory positions are stated.

entities.name: Siegel Family Endowment
entities.notes: Siegel Family Endowment is a private philanthropic foundation founded in 2011 by David M. Siegel, co-founder and co-chairman of Two Sigma, a financial sciences company. Its mission is to understand and shape the impact of technology on society, with grantmaking focused at the intersections of learning, workforce, and infrastructure. The foundation has made significant commitments to AI and technology policy, including a $6.5 million three-year commitment to institutions conducting technology policy research, with grantees including the Center for American Progress, MIT, Princeton's Center for Information Technology Policy, Data & Society, Aspen Digital, Washington Center for Equitable Growth, and UNC's Center for Information Technology and Public Life. The endowment operates a Partner Research Fellows program, now in its fourth cohort (2024-2025), which places researchers at partner institutions including CAP — the connection through which Nicole Alvarez was identified as affiliated with the foundation. As of 2021, the foundation managed over $415 million in assets, with Two Sigma paid over $500,000 annually to manage its finances. Katy Knight serves as President and Executive Director. The foundation's grantmaking approach is inquiry-driven and inspired by the scientific method, reflecting founder David Siegel's background as a computer scientist with a Ph.D. from MIT's AI lab.
entities.notes_sources: www.siegelendowment.org; www.siegelendowment.org; www.siegelendowment.org; siegelendowment.org; dk.linkedin.com; infrastructure.siegelendowment.org; influencewatch.org; linkedin.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The foundation explicitly recognizes that 'the development and deployment of technology is shaped by public policy — either through direct action such as regulation, public investments, or tax policy; or through lack of action on issues such as antitrust enforcement or pursuing violators of labor standards.' It funds organizations developing policy options and educating policymakers across a range of technology issues, suggesting a balanced, active regulatory stance rather than either laissez-faire or restrictive positions.
entities.belief_threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Bias/discrimination, Power concentration

entities.name: Silicon Valley Community Foundation
entities.notes: Silicon Valley Community Foundation (SVCF) is one of the largest community foundations in the United States, established in 2007 through the merger of Peninsula Community Foundation and Community Foundation Silicon Valley. In 2024, SVCF granted $1.54 billion to more than 6,000 nonprofits and community organizations locally, nationally, and internationally; in 2025, total grantmaking reached nearly $2.1 billion. SVCF primarily distributes grants through donor advised funds (DAFs) held by over 1,000 individuals, families, and 60+ companies, making it a major pass-through vehicle for Silicon Valley wealth. It shapes the AI ecosystem as a funder: confirmed grants include $360,000 to Atlas Computing (since March 2025) and $610,000 to the Effective Institutions Project (December 2024), both organizations active in AI governance and policy spaces. SVCF's grantmaking spans community development, housing, early childhood, health, and capacity-building in San Mateo and Santa Clara counties, but its DAF infrastructure channels significant philanthropic capital from tech-sector donors toward AI safety and policy organizations. In 2025, SVCF also launched the Community Lifeline Fund, a rapid-response initiative awarding $590,000 to anchor nonprofits facing federal funding disruptions. SVCF is the largest grantor of charitable dollars in California.
entities.notes_sources: www.siliconvalleycf.org; www.svcf.org; www.svcf.org; www.svcf.org; www.hinchilla.com; www.svcf.org; www.svcf.org; www.svcf.org
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: SVCF does not have a publicly stated position on AI regulation as an institution. Its AI ecosystem relevance is inferred from its role as a philanthropic conduit for tech-sector donors funding AI safety and governance organizations such as Effective Institutions Project and Atlas Computing, suggesting donor bases with varied views on AI governance.

entities.name: Sony Group Corporation
entities.location: Tokyo, Japan (global operations including San Francisco, CA)
entities.notes: Sony Group Corporation is a Japanese multinational conglomerate that has built a significant AI research and development presence through subsidiaries including Sony AI (established April 2020) and Sony Research Inc. (established 2023). Sony AI's stated mission is to 'unleash human imagination and creativity with AI,' with flagship projects including Gran Turismo Sophy, a racing AI agent featured on the cover of Nature magazine in February 2022. Sony AI operates dedicated research teams in areas including Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning (PPML), federated learning, computer vision, audio-visual generation, and reinforcement learning. Sony Research Inc. collaborates with Sony Interactive Entertainment, PlayStation Studios, and Sony Semiconductor Solutions to advance AI across gaming, imaging, and scientific research. Sony has an active academic funding program, providing Research Awards to university researchers including Aaron Courville at Mila, supporting foundational machine learning research. Sony participates in top AI conferences such as NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, and CVPR, contributing both applied and fundamental research. Sony AI's President Michael Spranger also contributes to Sony's overall AI ethics strategy, indicating engagement with responsible AI governance alongside technical development.

However, no explicit public policy positions on AI regulation have been stated in the available sources.

entities.name: Stanford Impact Labs
entities.notes: Stanford Impact Labs (SIL) is a Stanford University initiative that funds and supports interdisciplinary 'impact labs' — teams of scholars, government agencies, nonprofits, and private sector partners working to generate rigorous evidence and scalable solutions to pressing social problems. SIL uses a staged investment model: Design Fellowships, Seed Partnerships, Stage 2 (Test Solutions, up to $650K over 3 years), and Stage 3 (Amplify Impact, e.g., a $3.5M investment in Stanford RegLab over four years). It shapes the AI ecosystem primarily by funding research on AI literacy, misinformation, and the societal impacts of AI — notably through twice-funded investments in Jeff Hancock's Empowering Diverse Digital Citizens Lab, which develops AI literacy interventions for older adults, adolescents, and diverse communities. In 2022, SIL awarded approximately $4 million to eight impact labs, and in 2025 announced new investments in teams advancing health, labor, democracy, and technology solutions including AI literacy. Faculty director Matthew Gentzkow (Professor of Economics) and Shelley Correll have been associated with SIL leadership. The organization explicitly positions itself as filling a gap left by the comparative lack of federal funding for applied social science research, modeling how social science can discover and scale solutions to complex societal problems including those created or amplified by AI.
entities.notes_sources: impact.stanford.edu; impact.stanford.edu; impact.stanford.edu; impact.stanford.edu; impact.stanford.edu; stanford.io; momentum.stanford.edu; impact.stanford.edu
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: SIL does not explicitly advocate for specific AI regulation, but its funded research focuses on mitigating AI harms through literacy interventions and evidence-based policy — suggesting a moderate, evidence-driven approach to governance. It funds work aimed at informing public debate and policy rather than pushing deregulation or heavy-handed mandates.
entities.belief_threat_models: Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

entities.name: StepStone Group Inc.
entities.notes: StepStone Group Inc. (Nasdaq: STEP) is a global private markets investment firm founded in 2007 and headquartered in New York, NY. As of December 31, 2025, the firm oversees approximately $811 billion of total capital, including $220 billion in assets under management, making it one of the largest private markets platforms globally. The firm went public on Nasdaq in September 2020 and operates across private equity, venture capital, infrastructure, private debt, and real estate asset classes. StepStone shapes the AI ecosystem through direct investment in frontier AI companies, including participation as an investor in xAI (Elon Musk's AI company), alongside other major investors such as Valor Equity Partners, Fidelity, and the Qatar Investment Authority. StepStone invests via primaries, secondaries, and co-investments, and closed its largest-ever VC secondaries fund at $3.3 billion in 2024 (VSF VI), providing significant capital to the venture and growth equity markets where AI companies are concentrated. The firm acquired Greenspring Associates, a dedicated venture and growth equity platform, in September 2021, substantially deepening its exposure to technology and AI-adjacent investments. StepStone's clients include sovereign wealth funds, public pension funds, endowments, and family offices, meaning its capital allocation decisions have broad systemic influence on which technology companies receive growth funding.
