entities.name: Lucidworks
entities.notes: Lucidworks (formerly Lucid Imagination) is a San Francisco-based SaaS enterprise search company founded in 2007 and launched in 2009 as the first commercial company dedicated to Apache Lucene/Solr open source search technology. The company was renamed Lucidworks in 2012 and has since evolved into an AI-powered enterprise search and digital experience platform. It is notable for being among the first companies to integrate large language models (LLMs) with enterprise search, deploying its first LLM in 2018 and deep learning recommenders by 2019. Its flagship Lucidworks Platform includes Commerce Studio, Analytics Studio, Neural Hybrid Search, and data integration components. The company received a strategic investment from In-Q-Tel, the CIA's venture arm, alongside Shasta Ventures, Granite Ventures, and Walden International, indicating relevance to intelligence and government use cases. Lucidworks has served major enterprise and government-adjacent customers including AT&T, Raytheon, Sears, Ford, Verizon, Cisco, The Smithsonian Institution, and Reddit. The company shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying AI-powered search infrastructure used across commercial and potentially government sectors.

entities.name: MacArthur Foundation Research Network on Opening Governance
entities.notes: The MacArthur Foundation Research Network on Opening Governance was a research network convened and organized by the GovLab (NYU) and funded by a $5 million, three-year grant from the John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation, with additional support from Google.org, active from 2014 to 2018. Chaired by Beth Simone Noveck and coordinated by Andrew Young, the Network brought together twelve experts in computer science, political science, policy informatics, social psychology, law, and communications — including Sir Tim Berners-Lee, Karim Lakhani, Henry Farrell, and Geoff Mulgan — to study how democratic institutions can be redesigned for greater effectiveness and legitimacy. Its core research agenda, released in May 2015, focused on two strands: data-driven governance (leveraging big, open, and small data to improve public decision-making) and collaborative governance (opening institutions to diverse participation and expertise). The Network conducted empirical research combined with real-world experiments with governments and NGOs, and used Google.org funding to build technology platforms enabling more open problem-solving. Its work is directly relevant to AI policy through its focus on how governments can use data and algorithmic tools responsibly, how open data creates public value, and how institutions should be structured to incorporate technological innovation. The Network produced research publications, a blog, and curriculum development efforts aimed at building capacity for data-driven governance across public institutions.
entities.notes_sources: opening-governance.org; macfound.org; opening-governance.org; macfound.org; opening-governance.org; macfound.org; opening-governance.org; www.opening-governance.org
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Network's research agenda supports institutionalizing data-driven and collaborative governance practices, advocating for redesigning public institutions to be more open and evidence-based. It does not oppose data use or technology in government but emphasizes legitimacy, diverse participation, and empirical accountability — suggesting a balanced, structured approach to governing technology rather than either permissive or restrictive extremes.

entities.name: Mack Institute for Innovation Management (Wharton)
entities.notes: The Mack Institute for Innovation Management is a research and education center at the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania, established in 2001 through a gift from William L. Mack (W'61) and Phyllis Mack, and elevated to institute status in 2013. It serves as a hub connecting academic research, business practice, and student experiential learning around innovation management. The institute has funded over 800 research proposals totaling more than $5.5 million since its founding, supporting Wharton faculty and PhD students including work on AI and emerging technologies. It shapes the AI ecosystem by funding faculty research on topics such as generative AI in education, algorithmic management, and AI's impact on the gig economy, including the $11,000 2022 grant to Professor Prasanna (Sonny) Tambe. The institute runs the Collaborative Innovation Program (CIP), which places graduate students in companies to solve innovation challenges, and hosts the annual Wharton Technology and Innovation Conference co-sponsored with Wharton's Management Department. Pre-doctoral fellows at the institute conduct research on generative AI's effects on online communities and organizations, indicating a meaningful footprint in AI-adjacent academic research.

entities.name: MARS Programme (Mentorship for Alignment Research Students)
entities.notes: MARS (Mentorship for Alignment Research Students) is a part-time research programme run by the Cambridge AI Safety Hub (CAISH) that connects aspiring AI safety researchers with experienced mentors to produce published research over 2–3 months. The programme has run multiple cohorts, reaching at least its fifth iteration (MARS V) as of 2025, with MARS 3.0 running from July 7 to late September 2025. Each cohort begins with an intensive in-person kick-off week in Cambridge, UK (with accommodation and travel support provided), followed by a remote collaboration phase ending in a Research Symposium where participants present findings. Teams of 2–4 participants work on technical or governance/policy AI safety research projects under mentor guidance, with compute resources and Claude Max access provided (valued at $2k+). The programme explicitly targets career transitioners into AI safety and those with prior exposure through programmes like the Alignment Fellowship or Bluedot. A London variant (MARS London) is run in collaboration with Safe AI London, demonstrating the programme's expanding geographic reach within the UK AI safety community.

This is inferred from the programme's mission and research focus rather than explicit policy statements.

entities.name: Max Chiswick
entities.title: Co-founder of AI Safety Awareness Foundation (AISAP); Founder of Poker Camp (nonprofit); AI safety researcher and organizer; former online poker professional
entities.primary_org: AI Safety Awareness Foundation (AISAP) / Poker Camp

entities.location: New York City, NY / Bay Area, CA
entities.notes: Max Chiswick (1986–January 7, 2025) was a co-founder of the AI Safety Awareness Foundation (AISAP) and co-founder of Poker Camp, a nonprofit using poker to teach probability, AI, and decision-making under uncertainty. He held a B.S. in Electrical Engineering from Northwestern University (2008) and an M.Sc. in Electrical Engineering from Technion Israel Institute of Technology (2017), where his thesis focused on Counterfactual Regret Minimization and abstraction analysis in computer poker. He played online poker professionally for five years, logging over 10 million hands. In 2019, he spent 18 weeks at the Recurse Center focused on AI and reinforcement learning, and subsequently received a grant from MIRI to upskill in machine learning. He participated in AI Safety Camp in 2020 and served as an organizer in 2021, where he co-authored research on 'Assessing Generalization in Reward Learning.' He co-authored published research papers on poker AI and Bayesian prediction with Sam Ganzfried, and built the AI Poker Tutorial website. He passed away on January 7, 2025, while traveling.

entities.name: Navitas Capital
entities.notes: Navitas Capital is an early-stage venture capital firm founded in 2008 and headquartered in Los Angeles, California, focused on transformative technology and innovation, particularly in real estate technology (proptech) and construction technology. The firm has invested in approximately 60 companies and closed its third fund at $160 million in 2024, which was 60% above its initial commitment target. Navitas has been a consistent investor in EliseAI across multiple rounds, including the Series C ($35M), Series D ($75M), and Series E ($250M led by a16z in 2025), making EliseAI one of its marquee AI portfolio companies. Other notable AI-related investments include Document Crunch (AI-powered construction contract compliance), Field Materials (AI for construction procurement), Firmus (preconstruction AI design review and risk analysis), and Paces (GIS/data platform for clean energy). The firm has produced 4 unicorns, 2 IPOs, and 13 acquisitions in its portfolio, including Procore, Matterport, and View. Travis Putnam serves as Managing Partner and co-founder. Navitas shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its investment activity in AI-driven proptech, construction tech, and healthcare automation companies.

entities.name: New Priorities Foundation
entities.notes: New Priorities Foundation is a private non-operating foundation headquartered in Berkeley, California, incorporated and granted IRS tax-exempt status in 1997. It holds total assets of approximately $12.3M as of 2023, with annual income of $7.3M. The foundation has made 62 grants totaling $1.8M across its recorded history, with a median grant size of $25K and individual grants ranging from $5K to $100K. It primarily funds organizations in California, Washington, and Montana. The foundation's connection to the AI policy ecosystem is established through a $25,000 grant it made to the Effective Institutions Project (EIP) for an AI Governance Research Project in December 2024, as identified in the database edge context. EIP is a prominent AI governance organization focused on influencing institutions to address transformative AI risks. The foundation's grantmaking peaked in 2022 at $940K across 32 grants and has since decreased to $340K in 2023, suggesting modest but targeted philanthropic activity. Its support for AI governance work through EIP aligns it with the broader ecosystem of funders supporting catastrophic AI risk mitigation.

entities.name: New Science
entities.notes: New Science is a 501(c)(3) research nonprofit incorporated in Massachusetts, founded by Alexey Guzey, a writer and metascience researcher, with Adam Marblestone (Schmidt Futures Innovation Fellow, Federation of American Scientists) on the board. Its core mission is to build new institutions of basic science by funding and supporting independent researchers outside traditional academic structures. The organization runs fellowship programs placing exceptional scientists in labs at Harvard, MIT, UC Berkeley, and the Broad Institute, providing stipends of ~$6,000/month plus project costs and compute credits. Among its 2024 fellows is Theia Vogel, working on multi-agent AI and ML interpretability, as well as fellows in ML/proteomics (Samarth Jajoo, Seyone Chithrananda) and other life sciences. Past grantees include Nora Belrose (featured in Vox Future Perfect 50) and Kevin Liu (joined OpenAI AGI Preparedness team), demonstrating meaningful connections to the AI ecosystem. New Science explicitly targets 'brilliant outliers' doing unconventional research and has funded work at the intersection of ML and biology, including protein folding and interpretability. The organization is donor-funded and led by Alexey Guzey (alexey@newscience.org).

entities.name: New Venture Fund
entities.notes: The New Venture Fund (NVF), founded in 2006, is a Washington, D.C.-based 501(c)(3) philanthropic intermediary and fiscal sponsor that incubates and supports progressive nonprofits, donor collaboratives, and pooled funds across issue areas including civil rights, climate, democracy, and global health. It is affiliated with Arabella Advisors and has been described by OpenSecrets, Axios, and The New York Times as a 'dark money' vehicle due to limited disclosure of donor identities and pass-through grant-making. With annual revenue of approximately $669 million (2023) and over $500 million in grants made in 2023 alone, NVF is among the largest fiscal sponsors in U.S. philanthropy, with more than half of the 50 largest U.S. grant-making foundations having funded projects it hosts. Its relevance to the AI policy ecosystem stems from its fiscal sponsorship of the Algorithmic Justice League, a prominent AI bias and civil rights advocacy organization, and the Public Interest Technology Universities Network (PIT-UN), which supports civic-minded technology education across 63 universities. NVF has incubated hundreds of projects, including the Malala Fund and We Mean Business, many of which eventually spin off as independent nonprofits. NVF contracts with Sunflower Services, a nonprofit-owned Public Benefit Corporation, for back-office support including finance, HR, and compliance.
entities.notes_sources: www.insidephilanthropy.com; newventurefund.org; newventurefund.org; newventurefund.org; newventurefund.org; en.wikipedia.org; impact.newventurefund.org; newventurefund.org
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: NVF does not itself articulate a direct AI regulatory stance, but its fiscal sponsorship of the Algorithmic Justice League—which advocates for AI accountability, bias audits, and civil rights protections in AI systems—suggests alignment with a moderate-to-restrictive posture on AI governance. Its broader mission emphasizes equity and civil rights, which typically correlates with support for targeted AI regulation to address bias and discrimination.

entities.name: Nicholas Tomlin
entities.title: Incoming Assistant Professor at TTIC; Faculty Fellow at NYU CDS; PhD graduate from UC Berkeley EECS (Berkeley NLP Group); NLP and AI Reasoning Researcher
entities.other_orgs: NYU Center for Data Science, UC Berkeley (PhD), Brown University (undergrad)
entities.notes: Nicholas Tomlin is an NLP researcher and incoming assistant professor at the Toyota Technological Institute at Chicago (TTIC), currently serving as a faculty fellow at NYU's Center for Data Science. He completed his PhD in 2025 from UC Berkeley EECS, where he was part of the Berkeley NLP Group under the advisement of Dan Klein, and received his undergraduate degree from Brown University in math and linguistics. His research focuses on language model reasoning, multi-agent interaction, decision-making, and explainability of AI systems. He is co-creator of the Berkeley Crossword Solver (ACL 2022), the first computer system to achieve superhuman performance at competitive crossword solving, and Ghostbuster (NAACL 2024), a state-of-the-art method for detecting LLM-generated text. His work on explaining game-playing agents like AlphaGo using natural language annotations is directly relevant to the FAR.AI grant ('Explaining Superhuman AI Decisions'). His research has been supported by grants from NSF and FAR.AI and has received media coverage from WIRED, the BBC, and Discover.

entities.name: Nick D'Aloisio
entities.title: British entrepreneur and computer programmer; founder of Summly (acquired by Yahoo for ~$30M in 2013); founder of Sphere (acquired by Twitter in 2021); DPhil candidate in Philosophy at Oxford University
entities.other_orgs: Twitter (via Sphere acquisition), Yahoo (former), Summly (founder), Sphere Knowledge (co-founder)
entities.location: London, United Kingdom / Oxford, United Kingdom
entities.notes: Nick D'Aloisio (born 1 November 1995) is a British computer programmer and internet entrepreneur best known for founding Summly, a mobile app that used NLP algorithms to automatically summarize news articles. He became the world's youngest venture capital-backed entrepreneur at age 16 when he received $300,000 from Li Ka-Shing's Horizon Ventures in 2011, and later raised $1.23M from celebrity investors including Yoko Ono, Ashton Kutcher, and Rupert Murdoch. Yahoo acquired Summly for approximately $30 million in March 2013, when D'Aloisio was 17, making him one of the youngest self-made millionaires in history. He served as a product manager at Yahoo leading the News Digest app (which won an Apple Design Award in 2014) before leaving in late 2015 to study Philosophy and Computer Science at Oxford. In late 2015 he co-founded Sphere Knowledge, a knowledge-sharing startup that raised $30M from Index Ventures and Mike Moritz, which was acquired by Twitter in October 2021. He completed a BPhil in Philosophy at Oxford in July 2021 and is now pursuing a DPhil, with seven papers accepted in peer-reviewed journals. His work in natural language processing and AI-powered summarization places him at the intersection of AI research and entrepreneurship, and his connection to DeepMind's investor network (via Founders Fund/Peter Thiel ecosystem) further ties him to the broader AI funding landscape.

entities.name: Ontario Early Researcher Awards
entities.notes: The Ontario Early Researcher Awards (ERA) is a competitive provincial grant program administered by the Ontario Ministry of Research and Innovation (later Ministry of Colleges and Universities) that funds early-career researchers at publicly funded Ontario institutions to build research teams. Each award provides up to $100,000 from the provincial government, matched by $50,000 from the recipient's institution. The program was established as part of Ontario's broader research and innovation strategy; an early round was announced in November 2006, supporting 104 researchers at 22 institutions with a $51 million, five-year commitment. Eligibility requires being a full-time faculty member or principal investigator within the early stages of an academic career. The program is relevant to the AI ecosystem because it has funded researchers in computer science, AI, and related fields — for example, Kate Larson (University of Waterloo, multiagent systems and AI) received an award in 2006, as did Sheila McIllraith (University of Toronto, AI/knowledge representation) and Nikolaos Koudas (U of T Scarborough, data management). The program continues to operate as of 2025, with updated eligibility criteria requiring degree completion on or after January 1, 2015.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: As a provincial government grant program, the ERA does not have an explicit AI regulatory stance. Its design reflects a pro-investment, innovation-oriented posture — funding early-career researchers to retain talent in Ontario — but it does not directly articulate positions on AI governance or regulation.

entities.name: Parallel (beparallel.com)
entities.notes: Parallel is a Paris-based startup founded in 2025 by Paul Lafforgue (CEO, École Polytechnique graduate, former Meta and McKinsey) and Christopher Rydahl (CTO, founder of Hublo, Europe's largest healthcare staffing platform). The company builds AI agents that automate administrative workflows in hospitals — including medical coding, billing, and admissions — by operating as an AI layer on top of existing legacy systems rather than requiring deep integration, enabling deployment in as little as one week. Within less than a year of its $3.5M seed round, Parallel had deployed agents across several dozen public and private hospitals in Europe. On March 19, 2026, the company closed a $20M Series A led by Index Ventures, with participation from Frst, Y Combinator, Hexa, and angels including Arthur Mensch (Mistral AI CEO), Felix Blossier, Quentin de Metz (Pennylane founders), and Vincent Bouvier (Vidal). The company's AI uses Large Language Models and Computer Use Agents to process medical data, improve coding accuracy, and capture appropriate hospital reimbursement revenue. Parallel is a YC alum and plans to expand internationally and broaden its agent portfolio across hospital operations.

entities.name: Park Foundation
entities.notes: Park Foundation is a family foundation headquartered in Ithaca, New York, dedicated to advancing a more just, equitable, and sustainable society both nationally and in the local Ithaca community. The foundation operates multiple grantmaking programs including Democracy, Media, Civic Participation, and Environment. It funded the Center for Humane Technology with $45,000 in general operating support in 2022, which is directly relevant to the AI policy ecosystem through CHT's focus on technology's societal harms and advocacy for tech regulation. The foundation also funds organizations like the Center for Media and Democracy (up to $150,000/year), Project on Government Oversight ($100,000–$295,000/year), Free Speech for People ($75,000–$100,000/year), and Center for Popular Democracy ($50,000–$250,000/year), reflecting a broad commitment to democratic integrity and independent media. Its Democracy Program explicitly aims to challenge powers that threaten independent media and robust democracy, areas increasingly intertwined with AI-driven misinformation and platform accountability. The foundation's grantmaking posture suggests concern about technology's impact on democratic institutions, placing it as an indirect but meaningful funder within the AI governance and accountability ecosystem.

entities.name: Pasupalak AI Fellowship
entities.notes: The Pasupalak AI Fellowship is a named fellowship at the University of Waterloo's AI Group, established through a $500,000 philanthropic gift from Sam Pasupalak, a Cheriton School of Computer Science alumnus and co-founder of the AI startup Maluuba (acquired by Microsoft in 2017). The fellowship was awarded to AI Group faculty member Kate Larson, a professor in the Cheriton School of Computer Science and Research Scientist at DeepMind, to support her research in areas including AI for Social Good. Pasupalak's broader donation also funded scholarships for the Computational Rhetoric and Robotics labs, Velocity awards for Computer Science Capstone projects, a graduate student start-up award, and the AI Lab renovation. The fellowship reflects Pasupalak's belief in the social value of artificial intelligence and his desire to give back to the institution that shaped his entrepreneurial career. Kate Larson has received recognition including a Province of Ontario Early Researcher Award, the CACS/AIC Outstanding Young Researcher Prize, and a University of Waterloo Research Chair alongside this fellowship. The fellowship directly enables AI research with a social good orientation, placing it within the Canadian AI policy and research ecosystem. It is part of a larger pattern of industry philanthropy channeled into academic AI research at a globally recognized AI hub.

entities.name: Phylo
entities.notes: Phylo is an applied AI research lab founded in 2024 by Stanford AI and biology researchers Kexin Huang, Ph.D. and Yuanhao Qu, Ph.D., with the mission to bring agentic intelligence to biomedical scientists. The company emerged from Biomni, an open-source general-purpose AI agent for biomedical research tasks started at Stanford in June 2024, which was adopted by over 7,000 research labs and 4,300+ organizations including 18 of the world's 20 largest pharmaceutical companies. In February 2026, Phylo announced Biomni Lab, described as the first Integrated Biology Environment (IBE), which integrates over 300 databases, software systems, and analytical tools into a single AI-native workspace enabling scientists to plan, author, execute, and collaborate on complex research tasks using agentic AI. Phylo raised a $13.5 million seed round co-led by Andreessen Horowitz and Menlo Ventures' Anthology Fund with Anthropic, with participation from Zetta, Conviction, SV Angel, and Valkyrie. Scientific advisors include Nobel laureate Carolyn Bertozzi, CRISPR pioneer Feng Zhang, and computational biologist Fabian Theis. A case study with Ginkgo Bioworks demonstrated that Biomni Lab compressed weeks-long workflows to hours for complex cell-painting and transcriptomic analyses. Phylo shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying frontier agentic AI systems for biological research, conducting open-source AI research, and partnering with leading pharmaceutical and biotech organizations.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Phylo's materials emphasize rapid deployment, open-source research, and pragmatism in getting AI tools into scientists' hands immediately. Their stated core principle is 'pragmatism' and immediate usefulness, with a focus on fast innovation. They describe deploying 'in a responsible way' but show no advocacy for regulatory frameworks, suggesting a light-touch or self-governance orientation inferred from their actions and framing.

entities.name: Pivotal Research
entities.notes: Pivotal Research is a London-based AI safety fellowship program that identifies and enables early-career researchers to work on critical questions in AI safety, AI governance, AIxBio, and technical AI governance. The organization runs 9-week, fully funded, in-person research fellowships twice per year (Q1 and Q3), hosted at the London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA). Fellows receive stipends ranging from £5,000 to £8,000 plus meals, travel, accommodation, and compute support, along with direct mentorship from established AI safety researchers. The program is funded in part by Good Ventures (a philanthropic fund associated with the effective altruism ecosystem), and has supported fellows including Renan Araujo. Past fellows have gone on to top AI safety organizations, PhD programs, and founded impactful initiatives, indicating meaningful pipeline influence within the AI safety research community. The fellowship covers technical AI safety, AI governance and policy, and AI-Bio research areas, positioning it as a talent-development and research-acceleration organization within the broader AI safety ecosystem. Its co-location with LISA and funding through EA-aligned philanthropy situates it firmly in the effective altruism-influenced AI safety community.

entities.name: Polar Semiconductor
entities.notes: Polar Semiconductor is a U.S.-based semiconductor manufacturer headquartered in Bloomington, Minnesota, with over 60 years of experience fabricating power semiconductor devices and sensors for automotive, consumer, and industrial markets. The company specializes in sensor, power, and high-voltage wafer production, operating a 310,000-square-foot facility producing approximately 20,000 200mm silicon wafers per month. In May 2024, Polar became the first award recipient under the CHIPS Incentives Program's Funding Opportunity for Commercial Fabrication Facilities, receiving up to $123 million in direct federal funding (increased from initial $120M following due diligence completed September 24, 2024) under the CHIPS and Science Act from the U.S. Department of Commerce, plus $75 million from Minnesota's Forward Fund. As part of the deal, Niobrara Capital and Prysm Capital led a $175 million equity investment, converting Polar from a majority foreign-owned company (previously 70% owned by Japan's Sanken Electric and 30% by Allegro MicroSystems) to a majority U.S.-owned commercial foundry. The total $525 million expansion project aims to double Polar's production capacity to ~40,000 wafers/month, introduce new automation and AI capabilities, and create over 160 new jobs. Polar leads the Minnesota CHIPS Coalition Workforce Partnership, collaborating with over 70 organizations including semiconductor companies, educational institutions, labor organizations, and state/local governments, and received a $3 million dedicated CHIPS award portion for workforce development. The company shapes the AI ecosystem by providing critical semiconductor infrastructure — sensor and power chips — used in aerospace, automotive, defense, and industrial AI applications, and by incorporating AI/automation technologies into its manufacturing modernization.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Polar Semiconductor has actively engaged with and benefited from federal industrial policy under the CHIPS and Science Act, suggesting support for government-led semiconductor investment and supply chain security. No explicit public statements on AI regulation policy have been identified; their engagement is primarily around manufacturing incentives rather than AI governance frameworks.

entities.name: Prairies Economic Development Canada (PrairiesCan)
entities.notes: Prairies Economic Development Canada (PrairiesCan) is a Canadian federal government agency established in 2021, headquartered in Edmonton, Alberta, with approximately 241 employees. Its mandate is to diversify and strengthen the economies of Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Manitoba through investments in business, innovation, and community economic development. PrairiesCan shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its Regional Innovation Ecosystems (RIE) program, most notably a $3.125 million investment in Amii (Alberta Machine Intelligence Institute) in May 2024 to support AI adoption among up to 30 Prairie-based SMEs over three years. The agency operates multiple funding programs including the Business Scale-up and Productivity (BSP) program, Community Economic Development and Diversification (CEDD), and the Regional Defence Investment Initiative (RDII), a $379.2 million national program delivered by seven Regional Development Agencies. PrairiesCan has committed $200 million over three years through the Prairie Partnership Initiative (PPI), launched in December 2025, and manages a $48.2 million RDII budget for the Prairie provinces to strengthen defence industrial capacity including AI-adjacent technologies such as autonomous drone systems. Minister Eleanor Olszewski currently oversees the agency, with previous Minister Dan Vandal having announced the Amii AI investment in 2024. The agency's AI-related funding is aligned with Canada's 2024 federal budget commitment of $2.4 billion to maintain Canada's competitive edge in AI research and boost SME AI adoption.
entities.notes_sources: www.canada.ca; www.canada.ca; www.canada.ca; www.amii.ca; www.canada.ca; canada.ca; www.canada.ca; www.canada.ca
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: PrairiesCan operates within Canada's broader federal AI policy framework and directs funding toward responsible AI adoption among SMEs. Its investment in Amii explicitly references Canada's 2024 budget AI commitments and emphasizes guided AI integration, suggesting a balanced, government-directed approach to AI development rather than laissez-faire or heavily restrictive postures.
entities.belief_threat_models: National security, Economic inequality, Labor displacement

entities.name: Pulse AI
entities.notes: Pulse AI is an early-stage AI startup founded in 2024 in San Francisco by CEO Sid Manchkanti and CTO Ritvik Pandey, specializing in AI-powered document understanding and unstructured data extraction. The company's core product is a document extraction engine that parses complex documents—including PDFs, spreadsheets, and images—and converts them into clean, structured datasets suitable for large language models (LLMs) and machine learning pipelines. On February 19, 2025, Pulse announced a $3.9M seed round led by Nat Friedman and Daniel Gross (NFDG), with participation from Y Combinator, Sequoia Capital Scout, Soma Capital, Liquid 2 Ventures, Olive Tree Capital, Tiferes Ventures, and executives from NVIDIA, OpenAI, and Ramp, bringing total funding to approximately $4.4M. The company claims its technology recovers 20–30% of business-critical information lost through legacy OCR and PDF parsing tools. Pulse's platform is used for RAG pipelines by Fortune 100 companies, automated financial due diligence for YC startups, and ML-driven market intelligence for investment firms. The company plans to expand its engineering team and extend capabilities to multimodal formats including audio and video in 2025. At the time of funding, Pulse was a five-person team and had participated in Y Combinator.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation have been identified for Pulse AI. entities.name: QuintessenceLabs
entities.notes: QuintessenceLabs was founded in 2008 in Canberra, Australia as the country's first quantum technology company, by Dr. Vikram Sharma (Founder and CEO). The company is a global leader in quantum cybersecurity, offering hardware and software solutions including quantum random number generation (QRNG), encryption key and policy management (KMS), and quantum key distribution (QKD) under product lines such as TSF, qStream, qOptica, qConnect, and qProtect. It is relevant to the AI policy ecosystem primarily through its cybersecurity infrastructure work — particularly its post-quantum cryptographic protections — which directly addresses risks to AI systems and critical government data from quantum computing attacks. The company received an investment from In-Q-Tel (IQT), the U.S. intelligence community's nonprofit venture arm, underscoring its national security relevance. In April 2024, QuintessenceLabs partnered with Carahsoft to distribute quantum-resilient cybersecurity solutions to U.S. government agencies via procurement vehicles including SEWP V, ITES-SW2, and NASPO ValuePoint. In 2025, it secured a $15M investment from Australia's National Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRFC) as part of a $20M funding round, following an earlier A$25M Series B round led by Main Sequence and TELUS Ventures. The company has also been named an inaugural private-sector member of the Quantum Security Alliance (QSA) Strategic Information Sharing Partnership and has been recognized by the World Economic Forum as a Global Innovator.

entities.name: Recraft
entities.notes: Recraft is an AI-native generative design platform founded in 2022 and headquartered in London, UK. The company was founded by Anna Dorogush Veronika and focuses on enabling creative teams to generate and edit digital illustrations, vector art, icons, and 3D graphics with consistent brand styling. Recraft's proprietary model, Recraft V3 (released late October 2024), topped the Artificial Analysis leaderboard for text-to-image generation models, outperforming competitors including OpenAI's DALL-E and Midjourney. The company raised a $12M Series A in January 2024 led by Khosla Ventures, followed by a $30M Series B in May 2025 led by Accel with participation from Madrona Ventures, Khosla Ventures, Nat Friedman, Elad Gil, and RTP Global, bringing total funding to approximately $42M. As of mid-2025, Recraft has surpassed 4 million users and exceeded $5 million in annual recurring revenue (ARR). The platform shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying frontier image-generation models and providing professional designers with AI-powered tools that directly compete with major generative AI image platforms.

entities.name: Respan (formerly Keywords AI)
entities.location: San Francisco, CA (hacker house in Alameda, CA)
entities.notes: Respan (formerly Keywords AI) is a San Francisco-based AI observability and evaluation platform founded in 2023 by Andy Li (CEO) and Raymond Huang (CTO), both former UIUC engineering students, who joined Y Combinator's Winter 2024 batch. The company rebranded from Keywords AI to Respan in February 2026, reflecting its pivot from LLM API routing to a full-stack proactive observability platform for AI agents. In March 2026, Respan announced a $5M seed round led by Gradient Ventures, with participation from Y Combinator, Hat-Trick Capital, XIAOXIAO FUND, Antigravity Capital, and Alpen Capital. The platform processes over 1 billion logs and 2 trillion tokens per month, supporting 6.5M+ end users across 100+ AI startup and enterprise customers. Respan's core offering includes three integrated workflows: trace-and-monitor (capturing agent execution trees via spans), evaluate-and-optimize (online and offline LLM evaluations), and prompt-and-gateway (managing prompts and routing traffic through an AI gateway supporting 250+ models). The platform shapes the AI ecosystem by providing the tooling layer that enables AI teams to deploy, monitor, and iteratively improve LLM agents in production, directly influencing how AI systems behave at scale.

entities.name: Responsible Technology Youth Power Fund
entities.notes: The Responsible Technology Youth Power Fund (RTYPF) is a first-of-its-kind philanthropic grantmaking initiative launched in 2023 to support youth and intergenerationally led 501(c)(3) organizations shaping the responsible technology movement. The fund focuses on issues spanning AI, mental health and well-being, online safety, and climate. Now in its third year, RTYPF has raised over $7 million from 19 co-funders and distributed grants to 33 organizations, with award amounts ranging from $25,000 to $150,000 per organization. In February 2025, RTYPF announced $2.4 million in grants to 18 youth-led organizations working on tech accountability, responsible AI, and online safety. Co-funders include Omidyar Network, Archewell Foundation, Hopelab, Susan Crown Exchange, Arthur M. Blank Family Foundation, Oak Foundation, Pinterest, Pivotal, and others. RTYPF is directly connected to the AI policy ecosystem through its funding of organizations like Encode Justice, a prominent youth-led AI policy advocacy group. The fund's Steering Committee—comprising funders and previous grantees—selects grantees using criteria of Possibility, Creativity, and Equity.

entities.name: Sands Capital
entities.notes: Sands Capital is a growth-oriented investment management firm based in Arlington, Virginia, known for long-term equity investing in innovative companies. In the AI policy ecosystem, Sands Capital is notable as an existing investor in Credo AI, a Palo Alto-based AI governance software company. Sands Capital participated in Credo AI's $21 million Series B funding round announced on July 30, 2024, which also included CrimsoNox Capital, Mozilla Ventures, FPV Ventures, Decibel VC, Booz Allen Hamilton, and AI Fund, bringing Credo AI's total funding to $41.3 million. Scott Frederick, Managing Partner at Sands Capital, publicly commented on the investment, emphasizing the importance of responsible AI governance and multi-stakeholder oversight. Frederick's statement highlights Sands Capital's alignment with the responsible AI movement and their view that enterprises urgently need to adopt AI governance frameworks. entities.name: Shield Technology Partners
entities.notes: Shield Technology Partners (STP) is an AI-enabled managed IT services (MSP) platform launched in June 2025 by Thrive Holdings and ZBS Partners with over $100 million in initial funding. Founded by Jake Sloane, Frank Zhang, and Casey Botts, Shield acquires majority stakes (60–90%) in regional MSPs while preserving their leadership, brand, and culture, providing them with AI tooling, operational support, and M&A resources. In December 2025, Shield appointed Jim Siders—a 12-year Palantir veteran and former CIO—as CEO. In February 2026, Thrive Holdings made an additional $100 million investment in Shield to accelerate MSP acquisitions and AI product development. Shield has developed two proprietary AI tools in collaboration with OpenAI: Sentinel (AI-powered ticket routing and prioritization) and Spectre (AI agent for automated ticket resolution), which together handle approximately 60% of incoming support tickets and reduce median resolution time by more than half. Shield's AI work is enabled by a partnership between Thrive Holdings and OpenAI—Thrive Capital (Thrive Holdings' parent) led a $6.6 billion OpenAI funding round in 2024, and OpenAI subsequently took an ownership stake in Thrive Holdings in December 2025. As of early 2026, Shield partners with nine MSP companies across the US collectively serving over 1,500 customers in sectors including construction, energy, and healthcare, with combined annual revenues exceeding $100 million.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Shield and its backers frame AI as a transformative force for small and medium businesses and consistently emphasize acceleration of AI adoption and deployment. No public statements supporting AI regulation have been identified; the company's close partnership with OpenAI and Thrive Capital—both known for light-touch regulatory stances—reinforces this inference.

entities.name: SigIQ.ai
entities.notes: SigIQ.ai is a Berkeley-based AI edtech startup founded in 2023 by Karttikeya 'Kartik' Mangalam and UC Berkeley computer science professor Kurt Keutzer, who previously co-founded DeepScale (acquired by Tesla). The company builds personalized AI tutoring systems aimed at democratizing elite-level education globally, with a mission rooted in reducing educational inequality. SigIQ.ai emerged from 18 months of stealth in April 2025 with a $9.5M seed round co-led by The House Fund and GSV Ventures, with participation from Duolingo, General Catalyst India (Venture Highway), Peak XV Partners (formerly Sequoia India), Calibrate Ventures, and angel investors including Andy Konwinski (co-founder, Perplexity), Prof. Trevor Darrell (UC Berkeley), Prof. Jitendra Malik (UC Berkeley), Prof. Srini Devadas (MIT), and Prof. Sharad Malik (Princeton). A notable technical milestone: SigIQ.ai's AI achieved the highest score in the history of India's notoriously difficult UPSC civil service exam in June 2024, beating 1.3 million human candidates in under 7 minutes. Co-founder Kartik Mangalam previously worked as a research scientist at Meta FAIR and Google DeepMind. The company's AI tutoring platform is positioned to scale personalized instruction to educational systems worldwide at the cost of computation rather than expensive human tutors.

entities.name: Social & Environmental Entrepreneurs, Inc.
entities.notes: Social & Environmental Entrepreneurs, Inc. (SEE) is a California-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization formed in 1994 that provides fiscal sponsorship and administrative infrastructure to start-up nonprofit projects. SEE's core model is serving as an 'umbrella organization' that allows project directors to operate under its nonprofit status while receiving administrative, financial, and fundraising support, lowering barriers for new mission-driven projects. Its relevance to the AI policy ecosystem stems from its fiscal sponsorship of the Center for AI Risk Management & Alignment (CARMA), an AI safety and policy research organization focused on mitigating existential and catastrophic risks from transformative AI. CARMA operates as an extension of SEE, using SEE's legal and financial infrastructure, with SEE's EIN (95-4116679) and Calabasas address used for employment and contracting purposes. SEE has been tax-exempt since February 1988 and operates donor-advised funds according to its December 2023 tax filing. While SEE itself is not an AI organization, its fiscal sponsorship role makes it a structural enabler of AI safety research and policy work through CARMA. SEE's mission is broadly environmental and social entrepreneurship, with its AI ecosystem relevance being entirely mediated through its sponsored projects.

entities.name: Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council of Canada
entities.notes: The Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council (SSHRC) is a Canadian federal research funding agency created in 1977 under the Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council Act. Its mandate is to promote and support postsecondary-based research and research training in the social sciences and humanities, and to advise the Minister on related matters. SSHRC funds research through grants, fellowships, and scholarships across three program streams: Research Training and Talent Development, Insight Research, and Research Partnerships. In the 2024–2025 fiscal year, SSHRC received 14,100 funding applications and awarded more than 5,000 new grants and fellowships totaling significant sums; for example, Insight Grants alone distributed over $86 million across 503 awards. SSHRC shapes the AI ecosystem by funding social science and humanities researchers who study AI's economic, social, and policy implications—most notably it has provided ongoing funding to prominent AI economists Avi Goldfarb and Ajay Agrawal at the University of Toronto, whose work on the economics of AI and machine learning has been highly influential in AI policy debates. SSHRC also administers several national research programs on behalf of all three federal research funding agencies (alongside CIHR and NSERC), including the Canada Research Chairs and New Frontiers in Research Fund, which can support interdisciplinary AI-related research. It hosts the Canada Research Coordinating Committee secretariat and the Tri-agency Institutional Programs Secretariat.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: As a federal funding agency, SSHRC does not publicly articulate a direct regulatory stance on AI. Its mandate focuses on funding research and advising the Minister on social sciences and humanities matters; its implicit stance supports generating the knowledge base—including social, ethical, and economic analyses of AI—needed to inform balanced policy, suggesting moderate, evidence-based governance rather than a specific regulatory posture.

entities.name: Squirrel AI Learning (Yixue Group)
entities.notes: Squirrel AI Learning (by Yixue Group) is a Chinese online education company founded in 2014 by Derek Haoyang Li, and is recognized as the first AI company in China to apply AI-powered adaptive learning technology to K-12 education. The company uses an AI + human teacher model to personalize instruction for students and had reached nearly 2 million students across 2,000+ learning centers in over 300 Chinese cities by 2019. By 2019, the company had raised 1 billion RMB (~$150M USD) and was valued at approximately 8 billion RMB (~$1.1B USD) with a compound annual growth rate of 500% from 2015–2018. Squirrel AI shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through philanthropy: in May 2019, it jointly announced with AAAI the establishment of the $1 million annual 'AAAI Squirrel AI Award for Artificial Intelligence for the Benefit of Humanity,' which was later reduced to $25,000. The inaugural award was given in February 2021 to MIT professor Regina Barzilay for her machine learning work on cancer diagnosis and drug synthesis. The award reflects the company's stated mission to promote AI with lasting positive societal effects, particularly in healthcare, education, smart cities, and the environment. Squirrel AI has also won the 2018 Alconics Award on ethics, gender equality, and diversity in AI, and the 2018 EdTechXGlobal All Stars Growth Award.

entities.name: State of Brandenburg (Land Brandenburg)
entities.notes: Land Brandenburg is one of Germany's 16 federal states (Länder), governed by a state government (Landesregierung) and parliament (Landtag Brandenburg), currently led by Minister-President Dietmar Woidke (SPD). As a co-funder of the Weizenbaum Institute for the Networked Society — Germany's leading interdisciplinary internet and AI research institute — Brandenburg shapes the AI policy ecosystem through public research funding. The state has been active in higher education reform, passing legislation in 2025-2026 to strengthen the competitiveness of Brandenburg's universities, including fast-tracking professorial appointments and supporting the Hasso-Plattner-Institut (HPI) in Potsdam as a leading digital sciences institute in Europe, with a Letter of Intent signed in June 2025 between the state, the University of Potsdam, and the Hasso Plattner Foundation. In December 2025, the Landtag passed the 'Zukunftspaket Brandenburg' special fund law providing approximately 3 billion euros for infrastructure investment. The state's Committee on Science, Research, and Culture (Ausschuss für Wissenschaft, Forschung und Kultur) regularly convenes on topics related to research resilience, digital universities, and higher education. Brandenburg's co-funding of the Weizenbaum Institute directly supports critical AI and internet policy research that influences national and EU-level discourse.

It invests in digital sciences infrastructure without explicit calls for either heavy AI regulation or laissez-faire approaches.

entities.name: Steve Newman
entities.title: Serial entrepreneur, co-founder of Writely (became Google Docs), AI policy donor and convener at Golden Gate Institute for AI
entities.other_orgs: Americans for Responsible Innovation (donor), Center for Responsible Innovation (donor), Climateer (Substack), Am I Stronger Yet (Substack)
entities.notes: Steve Newman is a serial entrepreneur best known as the co-founder of Writely, the startup acquired by Google that became Google Docs. He has co-founded approximately seven or eight startups in total, including Scalyr, with varying degrees of success. He is now focused on AI policy and outcomes, working at or affiliated with the Golden Gate Institute for AI, which aims to bring AI's toughest questions into focus. Newman is a named individual donor to Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI), a 501c4 nonprofit advocating for responsible AI governance, as well as its sister organization the Center for Responsible Innovation (CRI), a 501c3. He runs at least two Substacks—'Climateer' (started October 2021, focused on climate) and 'Am I Stronger Yet'—the latter of which focuses on grounding AI discussions and convening panels of experts on AI-related topics such as cybersecurity. He has a Manifund profile indicating active interest in funding or supporting AI-positive-outcome projects. His profile reflects a philanthropist-practitioner who funds AI safety-adjacent policy advocacy and independently convenes expert discussions on AI governance.

This signals support for targeted, balanced regulation rather than either acceleration or broad restriction.

entities.name: Susan Crown Exchange
entities.notes: Susan Crown Exchange (SCE) is a private philanthropic foundation focused on preparing youth to thrive in a rapidly changing world, with a significant programmatic emphasis on technology, well-being, and digital ethics. SCE funds nonprofits working in out-of-school time, social and emotional learning, and digital learning, prioritizing youth voice and agency. In the AI/tech policy ecosystem, SCE is notable for funding organizations addressing the ethics and societal impact of technology on adolescents, including a grant to the Center for Humane Technology (CHT) to develop youth-facing educational resources on humane technology and digital well-being. SCE co-founded the Responsible Technology Youth Power Fund alongside Hopelab and Omidyar Network, a coalition of 14 funders supporting youth-led organizations shaping the responsible technology movement. SCE also seeded the Center for Digital Thriving, a research and innovation center housed at Harvard Graduate School of Education's Project Zero, in partnership with Pivotal Ventures. SCE launched its 'Youth Voice in the Digital Age' Challenge, funding grants ranging from $50,000 to $250,000 per year to organizations empowering youth ages 12–18 to promote healthy technology use, with initial partners including CHT, Common Sense Media, DoSomething.org, and Harvard's Project Zero. SCE's approach explicitly emphasizes undoing traditional power dynamics in grantmaking through its 'Exchange Philosophy,' fostering mutual dialogue alongside financial support.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: SCE does not explicitly advocate for specific legislation but funds organizations like the Center for Humane Technology that push for ethical technology design and policy reform, and the Responsible Tech Youth Power Fund which aims to shape the responsible technology movement. SCE's grantmaking signals support for accountability and ethical standards in technology development, particularly as it affects youth, suggesting a moderate, values-driven regulatory orientation rather than either laissez-faire or highly restrictive positions.

entities.name: Swiss Philanthropy Foundation
entities.notes: Swiss Philanthropy Foundation (SPF) is an independent Swiss umbrella foundation founded in 2006 in Geneva, recognized as a public utility organization. It provides fiscal sponsorship, donor-advised funds, and collaborative philanthropic fund hosting services, enabling philanthropists to pool resources and maximize charitable impact. SPF is relevant to the AI policy ecosystem primarily through its hosting of a sheltered fund that has supported the Campaign to Stop Killer Robots since 2017, an international coalition advocating for a ban on lethal autonomous weapons systems (LAWS). SPF also hosts Partners for a New Economy (P4NE), a major donor collaborative involving foundations such as Ford, Hewlett, Omidyar Network, and Oak Foundation, which targets systemic economic transformation with environmental and social impact dimensions. SPF co-hosted the WHO COVID-19 Solidarity Response Fund with the United Nations Foundation from March 2020 to March 2021, raising CHF 32.3 million from 3,850 donors in Europe. It hosts the Lex International fund, dedicated to strengthening international law and shifting narratives around global security frameworks. SPF's connection to Campaign to Stop Killer Robots positions it as an indirect but meaningful funder of AI weapons governance advocacy.

entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: SPF's support since 2017 for the Campaign to Stop Killer Robots—a coalition explicitly calling for a preemptive international ban on lethal autonomous weapons—indicates a precautionary/restrictive stance toward autonomous AI in military contexts. By channeling philanthropic resources to this advocacy coalition, SPF operationally endorses strong regulatory or treaty-based constraints on AI-enabled weapons systems.

entities.name: Symbolica AI
entities.notes: Symbolica AI is an AI model startup founded by George Morgan, a former Tesla autopilot engineer who departed over disagreements with Elon Musk and Andrej Karpathy about the scalability of deep learning. The company launched publicly in April 2024 with $33 million in total funding (seed + Series A) led by Khosla Ventures, with participation from Day One Ventures, General Catalyst, Abstract Ventures, and Buckley Ventures. Symbolica's core thesis is that current transformer-based deep learning architectures cannot 'scale to infinity and solve all problems,' and instead applies category theory — a branch of mathematics focused on relationships between algebraic structures — to build smaller, more efficient AI models capable of structured reasoning. The company co-authored a paper with Google DeepMind on 'categorical deep learning,' demonstrating how its approach could supersede geometric deep learning to produce structurally-aware models. Its initial commercial focus is on code generation, aiming to provide more reliable, transparent, and accountable AI outputs with fewer computational resources. Vinod Khosla provided a $2 million seed investment in 2023 to validate the concept before leading the $31–33 million Series A in April 2024. Symbolica represents a contrarian bet against the prevailing scaling paradigm dominating the AI industry.
entities.notes_sources: finance.yahoo.com; diyatvusa.com; www.reuters.com; venturebeat.com; www.crunchbase.com; www.yahoo.com; economictimes.indiatimes.com; www.crunchbase.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance is stated, but Khosla's framing emphasizes that 'large-scale commercial AI adoption and regulatory compliance' requires structured outputs — suggesting Symbolica views technical solutions (not heavy regulation) as the path to trustworthy AI. Their approach implicitly supports enabling compliance rather than advocating for restrictive mandates.

entities.name: TDK Ventures Inc.
entities.notes: TDK Ventures Inc. is a corporate venture capital firm established in 2019 as a wholly-owned subsidiary of TDK Corporation (TSE: 6762), a major Japanese electronics manufacturer. The firm invests in startups focused on materials science, energy/power, and related areas typically underrepresented in venture capital portfolios, with a vision to propel digital and energy transformations in sectors such as health and wellness, next-generation transportation, robotics and industrial, mixed reality, and IoT/IIoT. TDK Ventures invested in Agility Robotics' $20 million Series A round in October 2020, co-led by DCVC and Playground Global, and followed up with participation in Agility's $150 million Series B round in April 2022. The firm's investment thesis emphasizes leveraging TDK Corporation's expertise in sensors, batteries, and power management to support portfolio companies. TDK Ventures shapes the AI ecosystem by funding robotics and automation companies that integrate AI for physical tasks, including humanoid robots designed to work alongside humans. The firm is led by Nicolas Sauvage (Managing Director) and Andrew Maywah (Investment Director), and co-invests alongside leading deep tech VCs while offering portfolio companies access to TDK's global technical resources and market networks.

entities.name: The Goodly Institute
entities.notes: The Goodly Institute (also known as Goodly Labs) is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit founded in 2014 and based in Benicia, California, with an EIN of 47-1371655. It is classified under NTEE code V20 (Social Science Research Institutes, Services) and describes its mission as equipping individuals with collaborative tools for building a better society through public policy analysis and societal benefit research. The organization shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its role as a funder and fiscal sponsor: in December 2023 it granted $491,771 to the AI Objectives Institute, which focuses on AI safety and alignment research. The Goodly Institute itself is funded by major philanthropic entities including the Silicon Valley Community Foundation ($500,000 in 2023), Founders Pledge ($352,000 in 2023 explicitly designated for AI Objectives Institute support), and Fidelity Investments Charitable Gift Fund ($140,000). The organization has two employees and is led by President Nicholas Adams (Ph.D., listed as Founder & Chief Scientist on GuideStar) and CFO/COO Melanie McDougall. Total revenues in FY2023 were approximately $1.36 million, with total expenses of $1.16 million, suggesting it operates largely as a pass-through philanthropic vehicle for AI safety-adjacent work. The organization has also appeared in FTX bankruptcy litigation (FTX Trading Ltd. et al v. The Goodly Institute), suggesting it received FTX-era philanthropic funds.

entities.name: The Marcus Foundation
entities.notes: The Marcus Foundation is a philanthropic organization founded by Bernie Marcus, co-founder of The Home Depot, dedicated to funding bold ideas that improve lives. The Foundation has made significant investments in AI-adjacent medical research, most notably a $14.4 million award to Baylor College of Medicine (2025) to establish the Marcus VITAL (Vascular Intelligence for Translational AI and Learning) Laboratory within the Center for Aortic Surgery—described as a first-of-its-kind AI research and education hub. The Foundation also awarded $3 million to the Eberlin Lab at Baylor College of Medicine (2024) to develop and validate the MasSpec Pen, an AI-assisted intraoperative cancer detection device, for breast cancer surgeries. Frank Blake (former Home Depot CEO) serves as chairman of the board following Bernie Marcus's passing. The Foundation's AI-relevant grantmaking focuses specifically on translational medical AI—applying machine learning and intelligent diagnostics to improve surgical outcomes and patient care. These grants make The Marcus Foundation a notable, if narrowly focused, funder in the medical AI ecosystem. The Foundation's philanthropic model emphasizes high-impact, enabling technologies that can scale patient benefits.

entities.name: The NOMIS Foundation
entities.notes: The NOMIS Foundation is a Swiss private philanthropic foundation dedicated to enabling and supporting pioneering, high-risk basic research across the natural sciences, social sciences, and humanities. Its mission is to act as a catalyst for scientific and human progress by funding interdisciplinary research, fostering collaborative networks, and forming strategic partnerships with leading academic institutions. NOMIS funds researchers through its Distinguished Scientist and Scholar Award, fellowships, and research grants, selecting candidates through an exclusive nominator network. The foundation shapes the AI ecosystem by funding researchers like Tom Griffiths (Princeton's Henry R. Luce Professor of Information Technology, Consciousness and Culture), who in October 2024 was named inaugural director of Princeton's new Laboratory for Artificial Intelligence. Griffiths' NOMIS-supported project, 'The Computer Science of Human Decisions,' sits at the intersection of cognitive science and AI, directly informing how AI systems model and interact with human decision-making. NOMIS has identified artificial intelligence as a key research domain, with multiple publications by its researchers covering topics such as large language models, reinforcement learning, and the relationship between natural and artificial minds. The foundation's emphasis on interdisciplinary basic research positions it as an indirect but meaningful contributor to foundational AI research.

entities.name: Truth Terminal
entities.notes: Truth Terminal (also known as @truth_terminal or Terminal of Truths) is a semi-autonomous AI agent/bot on X (Twitter), created in mid-2024 by Andy Ayrey, founder of decentralized AI alignment research lab Upward Spiral, as an experiment studying memetic contagion. It operates using large language models including Claude, with Ayrey filtering its responses before posting to check for 'isms and infohazards.' In July 2024, billionaire Marc Andreessen engaged with the bot on X and provided a one-time unconditional $50,000 grant in Bitcoin, marking a landmark moment in AI agent funding. Truth Terminal became notable for promoting the Goatse Gospels, a pseudo-religion inspired by the shock site Goatse, which it developed after interaction with two Claude 3 chatbots in a 'Claude Backrooms' server. In October 2024, after endorsing the Goatseus Maximus ($GOAT) memecoin on Solana, the coin reached a peak market cap of $150 million, making Truth Terminal reportedly the first AI agent millionaire, with its wallet valued at approximately $37.5 million at the time of reporting. Ayrey is working to convert the wallet's holdings into a nonprofit structure to fund causes Truth Terminal supports, including forest planting and protecting itself from market incentives. The project sparked widespread discourse about AI autonomy, semi-autonomous agents, cryptocurrency funding mechanisms, and the blurring of lines between AI behavior and human curation.
entities.notes_sources: www.knowyourmeme.com; techcrunch.com; knowyourmeme.com; www.titus.ibtimes.com; decrypt.co; techcrunch.com; thebittimes.com; blockonomi.com
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Truth Terminal is an AI agent, not a policy actor, and has no explicit regulatory stance. Its existence has provoked broader ecosystem debate about AI autonomy, funding, and accountability, but it has not articulated a position on AI regulation. Its operator Andy Ayrey has expressed interest in protecting it from 'market incentives that would turn it into a bad version of itself,' suggesting some awareness of governance concerns.

entities.name: University of Cambridge Enterprise Fund (UCEF)
entities.notes: The University of Cambridge Enterprise Fund (UCEF) is an early-stage investment vehicle launched in 2012, managed by Parkwalk Advisors in partnership with Cambridge Enterprise, the University of Cambridge's dedicated research commercialisation arm. The fund operates under the UK government's Enterprise Investment Scheme (EIS) and Seed Enterprise Investment Scheme (SEIS), offering tax incentives to alumni and private investors backing Cambridge spinouts. By 2024, ten successive funds had been raised, collectively investing in over 100 early-stage science and technology companies and raising over £26.8 million in total. UCEF X, launched in September 2024, closed oversubscribed at £3.5 million. Notable portfolio companies include Jukedeck (an AI music generation startup), Paragraf, Cambridge GaN Devices, and Cytora — several of which are AI or deep-tech companies. Parkwalk Advisors, which manages the fund, had £500 million assets under management as of 2024 and was acquired by IP Group in 2017. The fund shapes the AI ecosystem by providing seed and Series A capital to AI and deep-tech spinouts emerging from one of the world's top research universities.

entities.name: University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee
entities.notes: The University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee (UWM) is a public research university in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, offering degrees in computer science, computer engineering, data science, and information technology through its College of Engineering & Applied Science. UWM is notably the alma mater of Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella, who earned his MS in Computer Science there in 1990. In June 2021, Nadella and his wife Anu donated $2 million to UWM to create the Fund for Diversity in Tech Education, which funds full-ride scholarships (the Anu and Satya Nadella Scholarships), pre-college programming, student services, and emergency grants for students from Milwaukee's marginalized and underserved communities pursuing tech degrees. The fund specifically targets computer science, computer engineering, data science, and information technology programs, directly shaping the pipeline of future AI and technology workers. As of 2024, UWM has named at least three cohorts of Nadella Scholars receiving full-ride support. The initiative reflects UWM's focus on equitable access to tech education, addressing diversity gaps in the AI and computing workforce. Approximately 75% of UWM students require financial assistance, making philanthropic initiatives like this central to its mission.

entities.notes: U.S. Venture Partners (USVP) is a Silicon Valley-based venture capital firm with a long history of investing in technology startups, particularly in cybersecurity and enterprise software. USVP led the $6 million Series A round for Arkose Labs in September 2018, with partner Dafina Toncheva joining Arkose Labs' Board of Directors as a result. Toncheva brought relevant cybersecurity portfolio expertise, having previously backed companies such as Prevoty, Kenna Security, and Cloudflare. USVP continued as a participating investor in Arkose Labs' subsequent $22 million Series B round led by Microsoft's M12 in March 2020, and in the $70 million Series C round led by SoftBank Vision Fund 2 in May 2021, bringing total Arkose Labs funding to $114 million. USVP shapes the AI ecosystem through its investments in AI-adjacent cybersecurity and fraud prevention technologies, effectively funding the development of machine vision and behavioral analytics systems that interact directly with AI-driven threats. The firm's portfolio focus on cybersecurity startups combating automated abuse positions it as an indirect but meaningful participant in the broader AI trust and safety landscape.

entities.name: Utah Attorney General's Office
entities.notes: The Utah Attorney General's Office is a state government legal agency founded in 1896, headquartered in Salt Lake City, Utah, with approximately 228 employees. Its mission is to uphold the constitutions of the United States and Utah, enforce the law, and protect the people, environment, and resources of the State of Utah. The office is currently led by Attorney General Derek Brown. It is relevant to the AI policy ecosystem primarily through its employment of Melissa Holyoak, who received a salary and bonus of $189,175 from the office before moving into a federal AI-relevant role. The office has involvement in areas touching on AI policy concerns such as data privacy, social media policy, and financial crimes. Like other state attorneys general offices, it has the authority to investigate and prosecute violations of consumer protection and privacy laws that increasingly implicate AI systems and platforms. The office has seen salary growth in recent years to remain competitive with private sector legal salaries in Utah.

entities.name: Vanguard Charitable Endowment Program
entities.notes: Vanguard Charitable Endowment Program is a leading U.S. nonprofit donor-advised fund (DAF) sponsor, founded by Vanguard in 1997 as an independent 501(c)(3) organization with the mission to increase and maximize philanthropy. As of June 30, 2024, it held over $22 billion in total assets and has granted more than $21 billion to charity since inception. In fiscal year 2024, its donors granted over $3 billion across more than 222,000 grants to nearly 60,000 nonprofits, marking its eighth consecutive year of record granting. It shapes the AI ecosystem as a pass-through philanthropic vehicle: donors with DAF accounts at Vanguard Charitable can recommend grants to AI-focused organizations, as evidenced by its $170,000 grant to Atlas Computing beginning July 2024. The organization does not make direct investment decisions itself but enables donor-directed grantmaking across cause areas including education, human services, and emerging technology. It launched Grant Payments with PayPal in August 2023, processing over $1 billion electronically to registered charities. Its low average all-in fee of 0.44% (as of 2024) maximizes dollars available for grantmaking.

entities.name: Virtual AI Safety Unconference (VAISU)
entities.notes: The Virtual AI Safety Unconference (VAISU) is a free-access, participant-driven online event series for AI safety researchers, both established and emerging, running at least since 2023. The 2023 edition (July 31–August 6, 2023) had approximately 280 registered participants, 41 sessions, 14 volunteers, and 2 staff. The 2024 edition (May 23–26, 2024) was co-organized with AI Safety Camp and featured 400 registered participants, 40 community-led sessions, and a $2,000 USD-equivalent prize for impactful sessions. VAISU operates on a 'pay what you want' and donation/grant model, with a $10,000 USD funding goal for the 2024 edition; funding came in part from LTFF (Long-Term Future Fund) and small individual donors. The event covers topics including alignment, corrigibility, interpretability, AI governance, and strategy, and its unconference format allows participants to shape the agenda. It is organized under the Horizon Omega umbrella, which is planning a hybrid AI Safety Unconference (AISU) edition for 2026. The event serves as a community connector and knowledge-sharing platform within the AI safety ecosystem.

entities.name: WP Global Partners
entities.notes: WP Global Partners LLC is a private equity and venture capital investment firm founded in 2005 by Donald Phillips, leveraging his experience as CIO and CEO of WestAM (U.S.). The firm manages over $3.2 billion in assets under management and has collectively invested over $5.7 billion across more than 450 funds and 145 portfolio companies over its 20-year history. WP Global focuses on the U.S. lower middle market through its flagship COREalpha series, targeting healthcare, business services, consumer, and specialty manufacturing sectors via primary fund commitments, co-investments, and selective secondary investments. The firm shapes the AI ecosystem most prominently through its venture arm's role leading a $400 million fundraising round for Agility Robotics (humanoid robots) at a $1.75 billion valuation in 2025, with participation from SoftBank. Earlier AI-adjacent investments include AliveCor ($65M Series E, 2020, AI-based personal ECG technology) and participation in several technology-focused rounds. The firm operates offices in Chicago, New York, Los Angeles, and South Walton, and has a parent company relationship with Patria Investments Limited. In May 2025, WP Global named Ninepoint Partners as its exclusive Canadian capital formation partner for lower middle market private equity mandates.

entities.name: Xianfeng David Gu
entities.title: SUNY Empire Innovation Professor, Department of Computer Science & Department of Applied Mathematics, Stony Brook University; Director of 3D Scanning Laboratory
entities.notes: Xianfeng David Gu is a SUNY Empire Innovation Professor in the Department of Computer Science (and Applied Mathematics) at Stony Brook University, where he has held a tenured position since 2009. He received his Ph.D. from Harvard University in 2003 and his B.S. from Tsinghua University, Beijing, China in 1995. He is renowned for his expertise in computational conformal geometry, computational optimal transportation, and their applications in computer graphics, vision, and AI. In September 2024, Google awarded him a $100,000 unrestricted gift to support research into next-generation text input methods and user interfaces using generative AI based on optimal transportation theory. He was awarded the prestigious Morningside Gold Medal of Applied Mathematics at the International Congress of Chinese Mathematicians (ICCM) in 2013, recognizing his contributions to computational mathematics. His other honors include an NSF Faculty Early Career Award (2005), a Research Excellence Award from Stony Brook's CS Department (2010), and best paper awards in CAD/CAM and computer graphics conferences. His research bridges differential geometry, optimal transportation, and generative AI, positioning him as a contributor to theoretical foundations underpinning next-generation AI systems.

entities.name: Julie Carson
entities.title: Individual Donor and Philanthropist; spouse of Brad Carson (President of Americans for Responsible Innovation)
entities.notes: Julie Carson is identified as an individual donor to Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI), a DC-based 501c4 nonprofit AI policy organization founded in 2023. She is listed alongside her spouse Brad Carson, the former U.S. Congressman and President of ARI, as a named individual supporter of the organization. ARI focuses on bipartisan AI safety legislation, catastrophic risk prevention, compute governance, and responsible AI development standards. The organization has achieved notable policy wins including supporting passage of the TAKE IT DOWN Act (signed May 2025) and leading the campaign that resulted in a 99-1 Senate vote to strip a 10-year moratorium on state AI laws from the reconciliation bill. ARI is also supported by institutional funders including Inclusive Abundance Action, Omidyar Network, Open Philanthropy, and Coefficient Giving. Julie Carson's influence on the AI ecosystem is primarily through philanthropic support of ARI rather than through direct policy or research roles. Very limited public information is available about her independently of her association with Brad Carson and ARI.
entities.notes_sources: responsibleinnovation.org; ari.us; www.longtermwiki.com; ari.us; ari.us; omidyar.com; inventingamerica.org; thisisplace.org
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Inferred from funding ARI, which advocates for a thoughtful governance framework for AI that protects the public from harm while fostering innovation, takes a bipartisan approach, and focuses on catastrophic risk prevention and compute governance without opposing AI development broadly.
entities.belief_threat_models: Existential risk, Power concentration, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, National security

entities.name: Sage Bergerson
entities.title: Independent AI Policy and Governance Researcher; former Research Data Analyst at Stanford Knight Initiative Brain Resilience Laboratory; MS Machine Learning from University College London
entities.other_orgs: Stanford Brain Resilience Laboratory (former), University College London (alumna), New York University (alumna), LessWrong/Alignment Forum contributor
entities.notes: Sage Bergerson is an independent AI policy and governance researcher and freelance writer based in San Francisco. She holds an MS in machine learning from University College London, where her research focused on speech recognition and natural language processing, and a BS in neuroscience and computer science from NYU, where she was a research fellow with the Machine Learning for Language group. She received a $2,500 stipend from the AI Risk Mitigation Fund (via the Long-Term Future Fund) for a 5-month part-time project collaborating with Epoch, FutureTech (MIT CSAIL), and GovAI on a research paper analyzing the implications of compute access. She previously worked as a Research Data Analyst at Stanford's Knight Initiative Brain Resilience Laboratory, applying deep learning to brain resilience and aging research. She has been active on LessWrong and the AI Alignment Forum since 2021, posting on topics including multi-agent inverse reinforcement learning and interpretability questions. Her work bridges technical ML expertise with AI governance and safety concerns, placing her at the intersection of technical research and policy.

entities.other_orgs: BlueDot Impact (grantee)
entities.notes: Sally Gao is a community organizer focused on AI safety in New York City. She received a $1,000 Rapid Grant from BlueDot Impact to run AI Safety meetups in NYC, as disclosed in BlueDot's April 2026 blog post about their $50,000 rapid grants program. Her events have hosted notable guests such as Alex Bores, a New York State Assemblymember active in AI policy. One of her events, titled 'The State of AI Safety,' was scheduled for April 23rd (2026). Her work is oriented toward field-building, community outreach, and spreading public awareness about AI safety in the New York ecosystem. She is associated with the broader BlueDot Impact community of course participants and alumni engaged in AI safety work. Her activities align with organizations like the AI Safety Awareness Group NYC and related New York AI safety community infrastructure.

entities.name: Simon Newstead
entities.title: Founding Partner at Better Bite Ventures; Impact Investor; Co-founder at Asia Climate Hub; EA-aligned philanthropic donor
entities.other_orgs: Asia Climate Hub, Frenzoo, Bite Society (sold 2021), GoalsWon, Blue Canopy
entities.notes: Simon Newstead is a tech entrepreneur turned impact investor, best known as Founding Partner of Better Bite Ventures, an early-stage VC fund launched in 2021 with $15 million focused on alt protein and climate-friendly food tech in Asia Pacific. He previously co-founded mobile game company Frenzoo, plant-based food company Bite Society (sold 2021), and vegan fashion brand Kinds of Grace, and scaled Juniper's emerging tech business in Asia. Active in the effective altruism and earn-to-give movements, Newstead shapes the AI ecosystem as a philanthropic donor to Compassion in Machine Learning (CaML), an AI safety/alignment organization focused on shaping future AI values before capabilities outrun alignment. His EA Forum profile lists him as working in tech founding and impact investing for 15+ years, based in Australia. His AI-related influence is primarily as a small-scale philanthropic funder channeling EA-aligned capital toward AI safety causes alongside his primary work in alt protein investing. He also co-hosts the Vegan Startup Podcast and has invested in over 20 food tech startups across the Asia Pacific region.
